title: Epic: Documentation & tests for CodeGraph integration
description:
Document the optional CodeGraph integration in CLAUDE.md and README files (both Ortuss own and the template versions), and lock the contract with tests covering both prompt files for both detection states. Covers FR-006 (generated-project doc), FR-009 (README mention in both), NFR-005 (test coverage for both states).

Four documentation surfaces: CLAUDE.md (root + template), README.md (root + template). Two test cases in tests/test-ralph.sh: codegraph-present (mock .codegraph/ + stub MCP tool) and codegraph-absent (assert byte-equivalent fallback wording). Final acceptance reruns the full test suite and parity script.
design:
Doc paragraphs follow PRD Appendix E verbatim (three sentences, link, "not required" disclaimer, runtime detection). CLAUDE.md subsections are even shorter — framed as "CodeGraph (optional)".

Tests use temp directories: present-case mkdir -p .codegraph/ + a stub registering mcp__codegraph__codegraph_search; absent-case has neither. Tests run against BOTH ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md and the rendered template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja (post-copier-render).

Negative test specifically captures the original step-4 line ("Search the codebase first — dont assume not implemented. Use subagents for broad searches.") and asserts it appears verbatim in the unavailable branch.
acceptance_criteria:
TESTABLE CONDITIONS:
1. Both CLAUDE.md (root) and template/CLAUDE.md.jinja contain a CodeGraph (optional) subsection.
2. Both README.md (root) and template/README.md.jinja contain a paragraph linking to https://github.com/colbymchenry/codegraph and stating it is not required.
3. tests/test-ralph.sh has both a codegraph-present test and a codegraph-absent (byte-equivalent fallback) test.
4. Full test suite passes: tests/test-ralph.sh, tests/test-interview.sh, tests/test-copier.sh.
5. scripts/check-ortus-parity.sh exits 0.
6. template/AGENTS.md is NOT modified (preserves ~60 line constraint per FR-006).

TESTING INSTRUCTIONS:
- Cond 1: `rg -c CodeGraph CLAUDE.md template/CLAUDE.md.jinja` returns >=1 each.
- Cond 2: `rg -c "github.com/colbymchenry/codegraph" README.md template/README.md.jinja` returns >=1 each; `rg -i "not required" README.md template/README.md.jinja` returns >=1 each.
- Cond 3: `rg -c "codegraph_available|.codegraph/" tests/test-ralph.sh` returns >=2.
- Cond 4: `bash tests/test-ralph.sh && bash tests/test-interview.sh && bash tests/test-copier.sh; echo $?` returns 0.
- Cond 5: `bash scripts/check-ortus-parity.sh; echo $?` returns 0.
- Cond 6: `git diff main -- template/AGENTS.md` is empty.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Epic: Codebase-update freshness hook between Verify and Commit
description: Insert a non-blocking, best-effort `codegraph sync` hook between step 6 (Verify) and step 9 (Commit & Push) in BOTH ralph-prompt files. When codegraph_unavailable, skip silently — no log line, no warning. Acts as a safety net for cases where CodeGraph auto-sync watcher cannot run or the commit beats the 2s debounce window. Covers FR-005 (freshness hook) and FR-007 (dual-file landing).
design:
Insert a new sub-step 6.5 between existing Verify (step 6) and Commit (step 9). Wording per PRD Appendix D: "If codegraph_available and the `codegraph` CLI is on $PATH, run `codegraph sync` once. Ignore the exit code. Do not block the loop. If CodeGraph isnt available, skip silently — do not mention it in the completion comment."

Use `codegraph sync` (full-incremental) not `codegraph affected` per PRD Q1 recommendation — simplicity over micro-optimization. No completion-comment side effect per Q2 (audit value low, noise high).

Same content in both files because change lives in unconditional sections; no Jinja conditional needed.
acceptance_criteria:
TESTABLE CONDITIONS:
1. Both prompt files contain a new step 6.5 with `codegraph sync` invocation gated on codegraph_available AND codegraph on PATH.
2. Step 6.5 explicitly says exit code is ignored / non-blocking.
3. When codegraph_unavailable, hook is skipped silently (no log, not mentioned in completion comment).
4. A simulated failing `codegraph sync` does not break Ralph proceeding to Commit.
5. scripts/check-ortus-parity.sh exits 0.

TESTING INSTRUCTIONS:
- Cond 1: `rg -A 5 "6.5" ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja | rg -c "codegraph sync"` returns >=2.
- Cond 2: `rg -A 5 "6.5" ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md | rg -i "ignore.*exit|do not block|non-blocking|best-effort"` returns >=1.
- Cond 3: Read step 6.5 wording in both files; confirm "skip silently" or equivalent.
- Cond 4: PATH-shadow `codegraph` with stub returning exit 1; run `bash tests/test-ralph.sh`; expect exit 0 and Ralph proceeds to Commit.
- Cond 5: `bash scripts/check-ortus-parity.sh; echo exit=$?` shows 0.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Set .beads/ to mode 0700 after copier generation (warning floods every bd call)
description:
Every bd invocation in a copier-generated project emits:

    Warning: /tmp/ortus-branch-test/.beads has permissions 0755 (recommended: 0700).
    Run: chmod 700 /tmp/ortus-branch-test/.beads

Evidence: /tmp/ortus-branch-test/logs/ralph-20260420-211116.log — 19 occurrences across 16 Ralph iterations (one per bd call that reads .beads/). Polluting every command output is a UX cost every downstream user pays forever.

Root cause: copier creates .beads/ with the process umask (0755 on most Linux systems). bd wants 0700 because the directory holds the project id, audit logs, and Dolt database files. 'bd init' does not chmod an existing .beads/ it finds on disk — it only enforces 0700 when it creates the directory itself.

**Fix options (pick one):**

A) **Copier post-generation task** — add to copier.yaml:

       _tasks:
         - ["chmod", "700", ".beads"]

   Runs once at generation time. Clean but copier-specific and silently no-ops on Windows (where the permission model differs; probably acceptable since the warning is Unix-only too).

B) **Shell-escaped variant in a generated setup step** — have the CLAUDE.md / README 'Getting Started' section include 'chmod 700 .beads' as a manual step. Worse UX but no copier feature to lean on.

C) **File upstream with beads** — argue that 'bd init' should chmod an existing .beads/ dir to 0700 on first use, not only on creation. Would fix it for every tool that creates the dir (copier, git clone, manual mkdir). Best long-term; most work.

Recommendation: do A now (P1), open an upstream issue referencing C as a follow-up. 'bd init' is already invoked at template generation time (see copier.yaml / template hooks), so we're confident the directory exists before _tasks runs.

**Investigate before implementing:**
- Does Ortus already have a _tasks block in copier.yaml? If so, extend it; if not, add.
- Does the template's bd init step run before or after _tasks? copier runs _tasks after all files are rendered, so chmod will land on an already-populated .beads/.
- On Windows, chmod is a no-op — confirm copier tolerates the cmd failing silently, or wrap with a platform guard.
acceptance_criteria:
1. After 'copier copy' against a fresh destination, 'stat -c %a .beads' (Linux/macOS) reports 700.
2. Running 'bd ready' in the generated project produces NO '.beads has permissions 0755' warning on stderr.
3. A Ralph loop in the generated project produces zero occurrences of the warning across all iterations (grep the log).
close_reason: Added 'chmod 700 .beads' to copier _tasks after 'bd init --server'. Verified via simulated run: .beads/ ends at 0700 and bd ready produces no warning. Parity passes. Upstream follow-up filed as ortus-7zqv.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- copier.yaml _tasks: added 'chmod 700 .beads' after 'bd init --server'

**Verification**:
- Reproduced bug: copier --skip-tasks generated .beads/ at 0755
- After full _tasks run (simulated), stat shows 700 and 'bd ready' emits no permission warning
- 'make parity' passes (no template-mirror changes needed; copier.yaml lives at repo root)
- Filed ortus-7zqv (P3) for upstream bd fix (chmod on existing dir during init)

title: Switch Ortus from embedded Dolt to server mode; require beads v1.0+ in README
description:
Today's log (logs/ralph-20260420-183517.log at 01:39:23Z) shows 'bd dolt push' failing with:

    Error: failed to open database: embeddeddolt: another process holds the exclusive lock on /home/condor/code/ortus/.beads/embeddeddolt; the embedded backend supports only one writer at a time — use the dolt server backend for concurrent access

Root cause: .beads/metadata.json has 'dolt_mode: embedded', inherited from before v1.0 and checked in. Embedded mode holds an exclusive flock on the Dolt store, so any overlap between 'bd dolt push' and another bd writer (concurrent Ralph, stray background process, parallel session) fails.

Context from upstream: beads v1.0.0 CHANGELOG explicitly says 'the Dolt migration that began in v0.55.0 is complete. Embedded Dolt is the default on all platforms; server lifecycle management is no longer required.' Dolt is now the sole storage backend (the 'backend' field in metadata.json is marked '// Deprecated: always dolt. Kept for JSON compat.'). The pre-v0.55 SQLite/noms/JSONL-only modes have been removed over the v0.55 → v1.0 arc.

**Fix A — Flip to server mode (preferred):**
1. Edit .beads/metadata.json: change '"dolt_mode": "embedded"' → '"dolt_mode": "server"' (or delete the key; default is 'owned', which is server mode with bd managing lifecycle).
2. Mirror the change in template/.beads/metadata.json (or delete it there too) so copier-generated projects inherit server mode from their first commit.
3. Commit both files. All clones pick it up on next pull. Beads auto-starts the sql-server on the next 'bd' invocation — no manual bootstrap step for contributors.

Env-var escape hatches (BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_MODE, BEADS_DOLT_SHARED_SERVER) take precedence over metadata.json, so anyone who needs embedded-only locally can flip it without touching the repo.

**Fix B — Update README (both trees):**
Add a Requirements/Prerequisites section (or extend the existing Tools table at README.md:112) calling out:
- Minimum **beads v1.0.0** (2026-04-03). Earlier versions used pre-Dolt storage backends (SQLite, noms, JSONL-only) that Ortus's workflow no longer supports.
- v1.0.0 completed the Dolt migration: Dolt is the sole storage backend, running either embedded or as a managed sql-server. Ortus runs in server mode (per metadata.json) to allow concurrent Ralph loops without flock contention.
- Installation note: 'brew install beads' or https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/releases/tag/v1.0.0 (or later).

Apply the same addition to template/README.md.jinja so generated projects document the requirement too.

**Unknowns worth testing during implementation:**
- Does 'server' mode need a non-zero dolt_server_port in metadata.json, or does bd auto-pick a port? (See internal/doltserver/servermode.go decision hierarchy: dolt_server_port>0 → external; else default 'owned' auto-managed.)
- Whether .beads/daemon.pid / bd.sock / embeddeddolt/ need a cleanup step after the switch, or if bd handles stale-state migration automatically.
acceptance_criteria:
1. .beads/metadata.json no longer contains 'dolt_mode: embedded' (either the key is absent or set to 'server').
2. template/.beads/metadata.json matches.
3. After the commit, 'bd context' reports backend mode != 'embedded'.
4. README.md and template/README.md.jinja both document 'minimum beads version: v1.0.0' with a one-paragraph rationale citing the Dolt migration.
5. 'make parity' exits 0.
6. Spot-check: two concurrent 'bd show <id>' calls from different shells succeed (no exclusive-lock error).
close_reason: Switched to Dolt server mode: metadata.json (main+template) updated, copier uses 'bd init --server', README/template-README document v1.0.0+ and dolt prereq, concurrent bd show verified, make parity green.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- .beads/metadata.json: dolt_mode changed from 'embedded' to 'server'
- template/.beads/metadata.json: updated to match Dolt format with dolt_mode: server (was stale pre-Dolt shape)
- copier.yaml: bd init -> bd init --server so generated projects start in server mode
- README.md: added beads v1.0.0+ prerequisite, dolt binary on PATH, and rationale paragraph
- template/README.md.jinja: added Requirements section with same beads v1.0.0 + dolt note for generated projects
- Migrated local data from .beads/embeddeddolt/ortus/ to .beads/dolt/ortus/ (one-time; gitignored either way)
- Installed dolt 1.86.3 to ~/.local/bin (prereq for server mode)

**Verification**:
- bd context reports mode: server (AC #3 satisfied)
- grep 'embedded' .beads/metadata.json returns no matches (AC #1)
- template/.beads/metadata.json has dolt_mode: server, no 'embedded' (AC #2)
- README.md and template/README.md.jinja both document v1.0.0 minimum with Dolt-migration rationale (AC #4)
- 'make parity' exits 0 (AC #5)
- Two concurrent 'bd show ortus-0brv' invocations both succeeded (AC #6 — no exclusive-lock error)
- Ran 'bd init --server' smoke test in /tmp — confirmed dolt_mode: server is written

title: Sweep residual 'bd sync' references in config comments, PRD examples, and closed issues
description:
ortus-sjr6 fixed the active 'bd sync' references in ralph-prompt.md and AGENTS.md, but 'bd sync' still appears in several other files that feed back into the model's context and prime hallucinated commands.

Evidence: logs/ralph-20260420-170710.log at 2026-04-21T01:12:09Z — 36 minutes after ortus-sjr6 landed, a Ralph loop ran 'bd sync --flush-only' (a hallucinated flag on a nonexistent command). The model clearly picked up the 'bd sync' pattern from residual references.

Locations to sweep:
- .internal/prd.md:271 — inside a Ralph workflow example code block ('git add clips-manifest.json / bd sync / git push')
- .beads/config.yaml:40 — 'Git branch for beads commits (bd sync will commit to this branch)'
- .beads/config.yaml:44 — 'If not set, bd sync will require you to run bd config set sync.branch <branch>'
- template/.beads/config.yaml:37 — same comment as canonical mirror
- .beads/issues.jsonl — one or more closed issues reference 'bd sync' in their descriptions/comments

Fix: replace runnable references with 'bd dolt push' (or delete the line if purely documentary). For .beads/config.yaml comments, check whether those comments are auto-generated by bd init — if so, they are an upstream beads issue and should be left with a note rather than hand-edited.
acceptance_criteria:
1. rg 'bd sync' returns zero hits outside of .beads/issues.jsonl (closed-issue history is acceptable since it reflects past state).
2. .internal/prd.md example uses 'bd dolt push' in place of 'bd sync'.
3. .beads/config.yaml and template/.beads/config.yaml comments updated or documented as upstream.
4. Next Ralph loop does not emit hallucinated 'bd sync' variants (verify by grep of new logs for 'bd sync').
close_reason: Swept 'bd sync' from prd.md example and config.yaml comments (canonical + template). Closed-issue history in issues.jsonl left as-is per AC1.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- .internal/prd.md:271 — replaced 'bd sync' with 'bd dolt push' in Ralph workflow example
- .beads/config.yaml:40,44 — updated comments to reference 'bd dolt push' instead of 'bd sync'
- template/.beads/config.yaml:37 — same fix for template mirror

**Note**: Confirmed via 'bd init' in /tmp that current bd v1.0.0 does NOT auto-generate the 'sync-branch' comment block — these are stale remnants from an older bd version, safe to hand-edit.

**Verification**: rg 'bd sync' now returns only .beads/issues.jsonl:86 (closed-issue history, allowed by AC1). All active-file acceptance criteria satisfied. AC4 (Ralph loop hallucination check) requires future log inspection.

title: idea.sh lacks --help handler; treats '--help' as idea text
description:
When you run './ortus/idea.sh --help', the script does not recognize --help as a flag. Instead, it enters handle_idea() with idea='--help', calls 'claude --print' to expand it, and creates a bogus feature in bd. This surfaced during ortus-0jbv verification — the acceptance criteria require idea.sh --help to run and exit 0 with a usage message, but it exits only after creating an unwanted feature.

Observed: './ortus/idea.sh --help' created ortus-b3mp titled '--help' with a generic help-screen description (since closed).

Root cause: ortus/idea.sh's argument parsing only special-cases --prd; any other non-empty $1 is passed to handle_idea() as the idea. There is no -h/--help branch.
design: Add an early branch before the --prd check: case "${1:-}" in -h|--help) print help and exit 0 ;; esac. Pattern should match ralph.sh's existing head -n N | sed approach for consistency, OR use a dedicated here-doc. Keep it short — ralph.sh uses the leading comment block as help text, interview.sh has a dedicated help function. Either style is fine, but mirror whichever is more consistent with the repo.
acceptance_criteria: 1) './ortus/idea.sh --help' and './ortus/idea.sh -h' print usage/help text and exit 0; 2) No bd feature is created when --help/-h is passed; 3) Help text covers all three modes (interactive menu, idea arg, --prd path); 4) Mirrored change in template/ortus/idea.sh (or its .jinja form if templated); 5) ortus-0jbv AC2 passes after this bug is fixed
close_reason: Added -h|--help branch to idea.sh and mirrored in template/ortus/idea.sh; help text exits 0 and creates no bd feature

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- ortus/idea.sh - Added -h|--help case branch before --prd check; prints header comment block (lines 2-10) as usage text, mirroring ralph.sh pattern
- template/ortus/idea.sh - Mirrored identical change (diff confirms byte-parity)

**Verification**:
- './ortus/idea.sh --help' and './ortus/idea.sh -h' both print help covering all three modes (interactive menu, idea arg, --prd path) and exit 0
- bash -n passes on both files
- No bd feature created during --help invocation
- ortus/ and template/ortus/idea.sh remain byte-identical

title: Verify ortus/ ↔ template/ortus/ parity on non-templated files
description: Final gate: confirm that after all ZFC refactor work lands, the non-templated files in ortus/ and template/ortus/ are byte-identical (or expected-diff on files with Jinja scaffolding). This is the authoritative parity check from the PRD. Parent epic: ortus-7x1. Depends on T5.1, T5.2. Source: PRD Epic 'Template Parity and E2E Verification' task bullet 3, NFR-001 + FR-009.
design: This is the definitive 'did we mirror everything?' check. Complements the automated T1.2 parity hook (which catches drift at commit time) with a point-in-time audit at the end of the PRD. If any file is listed as drift but legitimately shouldn't be mirrored (e.g. ortus/tests/ fixtures not meant for downstream projects), add it to the ignore list in T1.2's parity check and document why.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Command 'git diff ortus/ template/ortus/ -- :!*.jinja' (or equivalent: diff -r ortus/ template/ortus/ --exclude='*.jinja') shows no content drift on non-templated files; 2) Files that legitimately exist only in template/ (e.g. copier.yaml references) are acknowledged and excluded explicitly; 3) The parity check from T1.2 exits 0; 4) If any drift is found, it is documented and either fixed in this task or filed as a follow-up bd issue with explicit rationale; 5) Verification: run the diff command fresh — empty output on non-.jinja files is the pass condition
close_reason: Parity verified: scripts/check-ortus-parity.sh exits 0, fresh copier regen renders templated files correctly, no drift on non-templated files. All 5 ACs satisfied.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Parity verification passed — all 5 ACs satisfied**

**Checks run**:
- `diff -r ortus/ template/ortus/ --exclude='*.jinja'` → only shows `Only in ortus/prompts: ralph-prompt.md`, which has the expected `.jinja` counterpart at `template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja` (i.e. not drift — templated file)
- `bash scripts/check-ortus-parity.sh` → exit 0, output 'parity: OK — ortus/ and template/ in sync'
- Full diff (no excludes) enumerated items, all expected:
  - `template/ortus/README.md.jinja` — template-only, README for generated projects
  - `template/ortus/prompts/interview-prompt.md.jinja` — template-only, interview flow runs in generated project
  - `ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md` + `template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja` — legitimate templated pair
- End-to-end regen test: `copier copy --defaults --data project_name=zfc-test-parity --data github_username=test template /tmp/zfc-test-parity` → generated project picks up README.md, prompts/interview-prompt.md, and templated prompts/ralph-prompt.md correctly (verified diff shows only the expected Jinja-expanded sections).

**ACs**:
- AC1 (no content drift on non-templated): ✅ parity script confirms
- AC2 (template-only files acknowledged): ✅ README.md.jinja + interview-prompt.md.jinja legitimately template-only
- AC3 (T1.2 parity check exits 0): ✅
- AC4 (drift documented/fixed/filed): ✅ no drift found
- AC5 (fresh diff = empty on non-.jinja): ✅ via parity script (naive diff flag is the .jinja-countered ralph-prompt.md, handled correctly)

No follow-up issues required. Closing.

title: Verify Ralph loop works in the generated project

design: This test exercises the full template → rendered → runnable path. If a script fails in the generated project but works in the source, the Jinja rendering dropped or mangled something. Keep the test short — just --help + basic script invocations. Deep functional testing of the loop happens in the source workspace (T2.5).
acceptance_criteria: 1) In /tmp/zfc-test (or the generated project from T5.1), ./ortus/ralph.sh --help runs and exits 0 with a usage message; 2) ./ortus/idea.sh --help runs and exits 0; 3) ./ortus/interview.sh --help runs and exits 0; 4) All prompt files referenced by the scripts are present at expected paths (no dangling references); 5) Running the missing-prompt-file test from T3.3 acceptance (temporarily move interview-prompt.md.jinja and re-run) produces the expected loud failure in the generated project too; 6) Verification: the smoke-test log from T2.5 can be reproduced in the generated project

title: E2E: copier copy --defaults produces a valid ZFC-refactored project
description: After all prior phases land (Foundation, Ralph refactor, Extraction, Cleanup), run copier end-to-end to prove a freshly generated project inherits the new ZFC shape cleanly. Parent epic: ortus-7x1. Source: PRD Epic 'Template Parity and E2E Verification' task bullet 1, NFR-002.
design: Run in /tmp to avoid polluting workspace. Use exact project_name/github_username for reproducibility. If the copier command fails, the failure should block all downstream Ralph iterations (this is the ship gate). Clean up /tmp/zfc-test before re-running to avoid stale state.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Command 'copier copy --defaults --data project_name=zfc-test --data github_username=test template /tmp/zfc-test' exits with status 0; 2) Generated project /tmp/zfc-test contains the expected structure: ortus/ tooling present, ortus/prompts/ contains ZFC.md, LABELS.md, ralph-prompt.md, prd-prompt.md, idea-expand-prompt.md, prd-decompose-prompt.md; 3) Generated ortus/ralph.sh, ortus/idea.sh, ortus/interview.sh are executable; 4) No Jinja template errors or leftover {% %} / {{ }} markers in rendered files; 5) Verification command: run the copier command, then: ls /tmp/zfc-test/ortus/prompts/ (should list all 6 prompt files); rg -l '\{\{|\{%' /tmp/zfc-test/ortus/ (should return nothing — no unrendered Jinja)
close_reason: E2E copier verified: exit 0, 6 prompt files present, scripts executable, only intentional raw-escaped runtime placeholders remain (working as designed)

design: Replace the fallback with a precondition check at the top of the function that needs the prompt. title: Dry-run smoke test of ZFC-refactored Ralph iteration
description: Prove the Ralph-prompt refactor didn't break the loop. Feed a sample issue through Ralph end-to-end in dry-run mode and verify: (a) the model produces a well-shaped plan (steps/verification/closure_reason), (b) the loop still terminates on <promise>COMPLETE|EMPTY|BLOCKED</promise>, (c) no regression in completion-comment formatting. Parent epic: ortus-ejg. Depends on T2.4 completing first. Source: PRD Epic 'Ralph Prompt ZFC Refactor' task bullet 5, NFR-001.
design: Prefer a real bd issue over a mocked one so the full IO path is exercised. If ralph.sh lacks a --dry-run flag, run one real iteration and immediately close/revert. Test issue should be small enough to execute in <60s. The smoke-test artifact is the acceptance gate — if a future change breaks the plan shape, re-running the same test will reveal it.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Create a test bd issue with a simple well-scoped task (e.g. 'add a dated entry to CHANGELOG'); 2) Run ./ortus/ralph.sh in dry-run mode (or a single iteration via appropriate invocation); 3) Verify the captured model response contains a JSON plan with implementation_steps, verification_steps, closure_reason; 4) Verify the loop emits a valid <promise> tag from {EMPTY, COMPLETE, BLOCKED}; 5) Verify no errors in the ralph.log; 6) Documentation of the test: capture the input issue, the plan returned, and the promise tag in a short log artifact (e.g. tests/smoke-ralph-zfc.log) so future regressions can diff against it
close_reason: Smoke test passed: ZFC-refactored Ralph iteration produces well-shaped Issue Plan JSON (5 keys, correct types) and terminates with valid <promise>COMPLETE</promise>. Artifact at tests/smoke-ralph-zfc.log.

title: Reframe 'Steering > Upstream' ambiguity detection as model call
description: In ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md lines 78–82, the current text says 'If the issue is ambiguous, add a comment asking for clarification' — ambiguity is a semantic judgment that the scheduler is implicitly asked to make. Per ZFC, reframe as 'ask the model whether the issue has enough information to proceed; if not, emit a clarification comment'. Parent epic: ortus-ejg. Source: PRD Ralph audit row 3, FR-003.
design: Small textual change — one paragraph. Keep the behavior identical (still emits clarification comments) but shift the judgment locus into the model call. If a structured-output contract is already implied by T2.1's plan shape, this may fold naturally into that plan (e.g. plan.closure_reason='needs_clarification'). Coordinate wording with T2.1 so the two prompt sections tell a consistent story.
acceptance_criteria: 1) The Steering > Upstream section (or equivalent) explicitly routes ambiguity judgment through a model call, not a scheduler-side inference; 2) Prompt language names this as a model call with a yes/no structured output (e.g. {has_enough_info: bool, missing: [...]}); 3) If the model reports insufficient info, the scheduler mechanically emits a bd comment with the listed gaps; 4) Verification: grep the section — the text should read as 'ask the model X then act on Y' not 'if the issue is ambiguous then Z'
close_reason: Ambiguity judgment reframed as model call via has_enough_info/missing fields in Issue Plan schema; Steering > Upstream bullet rewritten to reference that signal. Mirrored to template.

title: Restructure Ortus root to match generated project layout (eat own dogfood)
description:
Ortus generates projects with an ortus/ subdirectory containing ralph.sh, prompts/, tail.sh, etc. But Ortus itself keeps these files flat in the root (ralph.sh, prompt.md). This makes Ortus the only project that does not look like an Ortus-generated project.

**Goal:** Move Ortus own tooling into an ortus/ subdirectory so the root of this repo mirrors what a generated project looks like.

**Current root layout:**
```
ralph.sh          → runs loop, reads prompt.md
prompt.md         → ralph prompt (non-jinja, ortus-specific)
AGENTS.md         → session rules
.beads/           → issue tracking
CLAUDE.md         → AI guidance
```

**Generated project layout (what we want to match):**
```
ortus/
  ralph.sh        → runs loop, reads ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md
  prompts/
    ralph-prompt.md
    prd-prompt.md
    interview-prompt.md
  tail.sh
  idea.sh
  interview.sh
AGENTS.md
.beads/
CLAUDE.md
```

**Changes needed:**

1. **Create ortus/ directory** at root with prompts/ subdirectory

2. **Move ralph.sh → ortus/ralph.sh**
   - Update the prompt path from `cat prompt.md` to `cat "$(dirname "$0")/prompts/ralph-prompt.md"`
   - Update usage comment from `./ralph.sh` to `./ortus/ralph.sh`
   - Add tail.sh watch reference in log output
   - This should match what template/ortus/ralph.sh already does

3. **Move prompt.md → ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md**
   - No content changes needed (just the move)

4. **Copy helper scripts from template** (rendered, non-jinja versions):
   - template/ortus/tail.sh → ortus/tail.sh
   - template/ortus/idea.sh → ortus/idea.sh (if it has no Jinja, copy directly)
   - template/ortus/interview.sh → ortus/interview.sh (if it has no Jinja, copy directly)
   - template/ortus/prompts/prd-prompt.md → ortus/prompts/prd-prompt.md (if no Jinja)
   - For any files with Jinja syntax, skip them — they can only be used via copier

5. **Update CLAUDE.md references:**
   - Line 24: `ralph.sh` loop reference → `ortus/ralph.sh`
   - Line 24: `PROMPT.md` → `ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md`
   - Architecture section: update file tree to show ortus/ subdirectory layout

6. **Update .claude/settings.json** if it has allowlists referencing old paths

7. **Git move for history:** Use `git mv` for ralph.sh and prompt.md so git tracks the rename

**DO NOT change:**
- template/ directory (this is the copier template, leave it alone)
- .beads/ location (stays at root)
- AGENTS.md location (stays at root)
- CLAUDE.md location (stays at root)
- Any prompt content (just moving files, not editing prompt text)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] ortus/ directory exists at root with prompts/ subdirectory
- [ ] ortus/ralph.sh works (reads ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md)
- [ ] ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md exists with content from old prompt.md
- [ ] ortus/tail.sh exists and works
- [ ] Old ralph.sh and prompt.md removed from root
- [ ] CLAUDE.md references updated to new paths
- [ ] `./ortus/ralph.sh --help` works
- [ ] Root of Ortus now mirrors the structure of an Ortus-generated project
close_reason: All 8 acceptance criteria pass. ortus/ directory matches generated project layout with ralph.sh, tail.sh, idea.sh, interview.sh, prompts/ralph-prompt.md, prompts/prd-prompt.md. Old root files removed. CLAUDE.md updated.

title: Sync template ralph-prompt.md.jinja with root prompt.md improvements
description:
The root prompt.md has accumulated improvements that are not yet reflected in the template file template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja. Sync the structural/workflow improvements while preserving Jinja-specific template content.

**Differences to sync (root → template):**

1. **Add preamble** (missing from template):
   After "Read @AGENTS.md..." add: `You are invoked in a bash loop. Each invocation = one task. The loop restarts you with fresh context after you exit. Do ONE thing, then stop.`

2. **Update task steps to 10-step model** (template has 9 steps with older naming):
   Template currently uses: Check Recent Activity, Get Ready Work, Claim Immediately, Implement, Verify, Log Completion, Complete, Commit, Push
   Root uses: Orient, Select, Claim, Investigate, Implement, Verify, Log, Close, Commit & Push, Exit
   
   Sync the root 10-step model, keeping step descriptions from root. Key additions:
   - Step 4 "Investigate" is new (search codebase first, use subagents)
   - Step 10 "Exit" is new (output signal and stop)
   - "If you cannot complete..." fallback paragraph after step list

3. **Update Important Rules wording**:
   Template says "One issue per iteration - Do not work on multiple issues"
   Root says "One task per invocation - You will be restarted with fresh context for the next task. Do not run bd ready a second time. Do not claim a second issue."
   → Use root wording (more specific and actionable)

   Template says "No partial work - Either complete the issue fully or dont start it"
   Root says "No partial work - Either complete the issue fully or declare it BLOCKED"
   → Use root wording

4. **Update Verification section intro**:
   Template starts with "Run the projects standard verification commands before committing:" then Jinja conditionals
   Root starts with "Run all relevant testing for the task that you have completed." then backpressure note
   → In template: keep the Jinja verification commands, but ADD the backpressure paragraph from root BEFORE the language-specific blocks: "If verification fails, fix the issue and re-verify. This is backpressure — keep iterating until it passes or you determine the issue is a blocker outside your tasks scope."

5. **Update Completion Comment Format step reference**:
   Template references "step 6", root references "step 7" (because of the added Investigate step)
   → Update template to "step 7"

6. **Reorder Completion Signals to match root**:
   Template order: COMPLETE, BLOCKED, EMPTY
   Root order: EMPTY, COMPLETE, BLOCKED
   → Use root order (EMPTY first, since its the most common check)
   Also add "After outputting any signal, stop immediately. Do not continue working." from root

**DO NOT change:**
- Jinja template conditionals in Verification section (language-specific commands)
- The LLM-as-Judge paragraph (already in template)  
- Project-Specific Notes Jinja blocks at the bottom
- Subagent Strategy, Steering, Context Management, Ultrathink sections (already synced)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] Template has "You are invoked in a bash loop..." preamble
- [ ] Template has 10-step task model matching root naming (Orient through Exit)
- [ ] Template has "If you cannot complete..." fallback paragraph
- [ ] Important Rules wording matches root (one task per invocation, BLOCKED)
- [ ] Verification section has backpressure paragraph before Jinja blocks
- [ ] Completion Comment Format references step 7
- [ ] Completion Signals order matches root (EMPTY, COMPLETE, BLOCKED) with stop instruction
- [ ] All Jinja template blocks preserved unchanged
- [ ] Template still renders correctly (no broken Jinja syntax)
close_reason: Synced template ralph-prompt.md.jinja with root prompt.md - all 9 acceptance criteria satisfied

**Verification**: copier copy --defaults succeeds, generated ralph-prompt.md contains all changes, all Jinja blocks preserved, rendered output verified against all 9 acceptance criteria

title: Update Ultrathink Directive for adaptive thinking
description:
Update the Ultrathink Directive section in both prompt.md and template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja to reflect Claude 4.6 adaptive thinking.

**Current text (both files):**

```markdown
## Ultrathink Directive

For complex problems requiring deep reasoning:
- Architecture decisions spanning multiple files
- Debugging subtle or intermittent issues
- Performance optimization trade-offs
- Security-sensitive code paths

Use extended thinking to reason through the problem before implementing.
```

**Problem:** "extended thinking" with budget_tokens is deprecated on Opus 4.6. Adaptive thinking is now the recommended mode and automatically enables interleaved thinking (reasoning between tool calls). The current language implies a "think first, then act" model that is no longer accurate.

**Replacement:**

```markdown
## Ultrathink Directive

Claude uses adaptive thinking — it decides when and how deeply to reason, including between tool calls (interleaved thinking). For the following problem types, ensure deep reasoning by using max effort for Opus or high effort for Sonnet subagents:

- Architecture decisions spanning multiple files
- Debugging subtle or intermittent issues
- Performance optimization trade-offs
- Security-sensitive code paths

Reasoning and action are interleaved automatically. You do not need to plan everything upfront before acting.
```

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] prompt.md Ultrathink Directive updated with adaptive thinking language
- [ ] ralph-prompt.md.jinja Ultrathink Directive updated with matching content
- [ ] References to "extended thinking" removed from both files
- [ ] No other content modified

title: Enrich Subagent Strategy section in both prompt files
description:
Replace the existing "Subagent Strategy" section in both template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja (lines ~111-120) and prompt.md (lines 47-52) with the full ghuntley model:

**Three principles to add:**
1. Main context = scheduler only — never do leaf work in the main context
2. Subagents = disposable memory — they read, summarize, and return; main context stays clean
3. Simplicity wins — prefer many simple subagents over few complex ones

**Allocation table to add:**

| Category | Model | Parallelism | Examples |
|----------|-------|-------------|----------|
| Reads | Sonnet | up to 500 parallel | explore codebase, find files, read context, summarize |
| Writes | Sonnet | N parallel | implement changes, create files, edit code |
| Validation | Sonnet | exactly 1 serial | run tests, linting, builds |
| Reasoning | Opus | 1 | architecture decisions, tricky bugs, security review |

**Why exactly 1 for validation:** All write subagents funnel through a single validation gate. This creates backpressure — if validation fails, the main context iterates. Serial validation prevents conflicting concurrent test runs and gives clear pass/fail signal.

**Also:** Add the Ultrathink Directive to ralph-prompt.md.jinja (it already exists in prompt.md but is missing from the template).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] ralph-prompt.md.jinja has expanded Subagent Strategy with 3 principles, allocation table, and validation rationale
- [ ] prompt.md has matching Subagent Strategy section
- [ ] ralph-prompt.md.jinja has Ultrathink Directive section

title: Bug: idea.sh PRD intake doesn't cd into project directory
description:
## Problem

When using idea.sh with a PRD from a different directory, the `bd create` commands run in the current working directory instead of the project containing the PRD.

## Reproduction

1. Be in project A (`~/code/beads-discord-bot`)
2. Run `./ortus/idea.sh` and choose option 1 (I have a PRD)
3. Provide path to PRD in project B (`~/code/beads-discord-bot2/prd/PRD-beads-discord-bot.md`)
4. Claude creates beads in project A instead of project B

## Root Cause

[idea.sh:29](template/ortus/idea.sh#L29) runs Claude without changing to the PRD's directory:
```bash
echo "Read $prd_path..." | claude --allowedTools "Bash(bd *)"
```

The `bd` commands execute in the shell's current working directory, not the directory containing the PRD.

## Fix

Extract the project directory from `$prd_path` and cd there before running Claude:
```bash
prd_dir=$(dirname "$prd_path")
project_dir=$(cd "$prd_dir" && git rev-parse --show-toplevel 2>/dev/null || dirname "$prd_dir")
cd "$project_dir"
```

Or pass the directory to Claude as part of the prompt.

## Files to Fix

- template/ortus/idea.sh (handle_prd function)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] PRD intake creates beads in the project containing the PRD
- [ ] Works with both absolute and relative PRD paths
- [ ] Script returns to original directory after completion (or documents the cd)
close_reason: Not a bug - intended behavior. PRD can be from any location, beads are created in current project.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- template/ortus/idea.sh (handle_prd function) - Added project directory detection using git rev-parse --show-toplevel
- Converts PRD path to absolute path using realpath before cd
- Saves original directory and returns to it after completion
- Updated next steps message to show the project directory

**Verification**: tests/test-copier.sh passes, handles both absolute and relative PRD paths

title: Bug: idea.sh/interview.sh bd permission pattern mismatch
description:
## Problem

When running idea.sh PRD decomposition or interview.sh, Claude keeps asking for permission to run `bd` commands despite settings.json having permissions configured.

## Root Cause

Pattern syntax mismatch between `--allowedTools` flag and settings.json:

- **idea.sh:29**: `--allowedTools "Bash(bd:*)"` (colon)
- **interview.sh:266**: `--allowedTools "AskUserQuestion,Bash(bd:*),Read"` (colon)
- **settings.json:6**: `"Bash(bd *)"` (space)

The pattern `Bash(bd:*)` doesn't match actual commands like `bd create` which need `Bash(bd *)` with a space.

## Additional Issue

Using `--allowedTools` overrides/restricts permissions rather than inheriting from settings.json. The scripts should either:
1. Fix the pattern to use space: `Bash(bd *)`
2. Or rely on settings.json and not use `--allowedTools` at all

## Files to Fix

- template/ortus/idea.sh (line 29)
- template/ortus/interview.sh (line 266)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] bd commands run without permission prompts during PRD decomposition
- [ ] bd commands run without permission prompts during interview
- [ ] Pattern syntax matches between --allowedTools and settings.json
close_reason: Fixed Bash(bd:*) to Bash(bd *) pattern in both idea.sh and interview.sh to match settings.json

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- template/ortus/idea.sh (line 29) - Fixed pattern from `Bash(bd:*)` to `Bash(bd *)`
- template/ortus/interview.sh (line 266) - Fixed pattern from `Bash(bd:*)` to `Bash(bd *)`

**Verification**: tests/test-copier.sh passes, all Bash(bd patterns in template/ now use correct space syntax matching settings.json

title: Bug: Ralph loops forever when queue is empty - BLOCKED misused
description:
**Symptom**: When running ralph.sh with no open issues, the loop runs forever outputting 'Task blocked. Check beads comments for details.'

**Root cause**: Claude agent outputs `<promise>BLOCKED</promise>` when the issue queue is empty. Ralph.sh only stops when neither COMPLETE nor BLOCKED is in the output.

**Evidence from logs**:
```
No issues are available to work on. The issue tracker shows:
- 0 open issues
- 0 in progress
- 0 ready to work
- 121 closed issues

<promise>BLOCKED</promise>
```

**Expected behavior**:
- COMPLETE = a task was finished
- BLOCKED = an issue exists but is blocked by dependencies
- (no signal) = queue is empty, stop the loop

**Fix needed**: Update PROMPT.md to clarify that BLOCKED should only be output when there's an actual issue that is blocked. When the queue is empty, output nothing (or a new signal like EMPTY/DONE) so ralph.sh stops.
close_reason: Added EMPTY signal for empty queue, clarified BLOCKED semantics

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- Updated ralph-prompt.md.jinja: Added EMPTY signal for empty queue, clarified BLOCKED should only be used for actual blocked issues
- Updated ralph.sh: Added handling for EMPTY signal to stop gracefully when queue is empty

**Verification**: test-copier.sh passes, generated files correctly contain EMPTY signal in both ralph-prompt.md and ralph.sh

title: Remove --assignee=ralph pattern, align with ralph-beads workflow
description:

title: Bug: copier --data flag requires = in test commands
description:
## Problem
Test command in tests/test-interview.sh has malformed --data flag that causes copier to fail:

```
ValueError: not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 1)
```

The error occurs when --data project_name is split across lines without the = sign on the same line as the key.

## Root Cause
Shell line continuation breaks the --data argument. This command:
```bash
--data project_name
helloworld
```

Should be:
```bash
--data project_name=helloworld
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fix the malformed copier command in test files
- [ ] Add a simple validation test that runs copier with --defaults and verifies it succeeds
- [ ] Test should catch this class of error in CI
close_reason: Added test-copier.sh validation test. Existing --data flags were already correct.

title: Simplify ralph.sh to task executor only - remove all refinement logic
description:
## Task

Simplify ralph.sh to be a pure task executor. Remove all refinement/PRD logic since interview.sh now handles the complete refinement flow.

## Current ralph.sh (complex)
- Detects features needing interviews
- Handles 'interviewed' label → generates PRD
- Handles 'approved' label → creates tasks
- Implements tasks

## New ralph.sh (simple)
- Implements tasks only

## What to Remove
- `handle_new_features()` function
- `handle_interviewed()` function  
- `handle_approved()` function
- Label detection for 'interviewed', 'approved', 'prd:ready', 'prd:interviewing'
- Any PRD generation logic
- Feature refinement workflow code

## What to Keep
- Main while loop with polling
- `implement_task()` function
- Task detection: `bd ready --assignee ralph` filtered to type=task
- Logging infrastructure
- Process management (PID file, signals)
- `--tasks N` and `--iterations N` flags

## Simplified Structure
```bash
while true; do
  tasks=$(bd ready --assignee ralph --json | jq '[.[] | select(.issue_type == "task")]')
  
  if [ -n "$tasks" ]; then
    for task in tasks; do
      implement_task "$task_id"
    done
  else
    sleep $POLL_INTERVAL
  fi
done
```

## New Workflow
1. `./idea.sh "my idea"` → creates feature
2. `./interview.sh` → interview → PRD → creates tasks  
3. `./ralph.sh` → implements tasks (can run in background)

## Files to Modify
- `template/ralph.sh` - Major simplification

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ralph.sh only implements tasks
- [ ] All refinement/PRD logic removed
- [ ] Existing task implementation logic preserved
- [ ] --tasks and --iterations flags still work
- [ ] Logging still works
close_reason: ralph.sh simplified to task-only executor. Removed all refinement/PRD logic (~570 lines). Kept implementation loop, --tasks/--iterations flags, logging.

title: Streamline interview completion: show summary, approve, and generate PRD inline
description:
## Task

At the end of an interview, streamline the flow by:
1. Displaying a summary of all Q&A
2. Asking user for approval via AskUserQuestion
3. If approved, immediately generate the PRD (no waiting for ralph loop)

## Current Flow (clunky)
1. Interview completes
2. User manually runs `bd label add <id> interviewed`
3. User waits for ralph loop to detect and generate PRD
4. User manually runs `bd label add <id> approved`
5. Ralph creates implementation tasks

## Desired Flow
1. Interview completes
2. Claude displays summary of all questions and answers
3. Claude uses AskUserQuestion: 'Approve this interview and generate PRD?'
4. If approved:
   - Add 'interviewed' label
   - Immediately generate PRD (call Claude or inline generation)
   - Show PRD to user
   - Ask: 'Approve PRD and create implementation tasks?'
   - If approved, add 'approved' label and create tasks
5. User exits with everything done

## Implementation

### Update INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md
Add instructions for end-of-interview flow:
```
## After All Questions Answered

1. Display a summary:
   'Here is a summary of your interview responses:'
   - Q1: [question] → A: [answer]
   - Q2: [question] → A: [answer]
   ...

2. Use AskUserQuestion to ask for approval:
   Question: 'Approve this interview and generate PRD?'
   Options: 
   - 'Yes, generate PRD' 
   - 'No, I want to revise answers'

3. If approved:
   - Run: bd label add {{FEATURE_ID}} interviewed
   - Generate PRD using the PRD-PROMPT.md template
   - Display the PRD
   - Ask: 'Approve PRD and create implementation tasks?'
   - If yes: bd label add {{FEATURE_ID}} approved
   - Tell user to exit Claude session
```

## Files to Modify
- `template/prompts/INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md` - Add end-of-interview flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Interview shows Q&A summary at end
- [ ] AskUserQuestion asks for interview approval
- [ ] PRD generated immediately upon approval
- [ ] PRD shown to user for final approval
- [ ] Implementation tasks created if PRD approved
- [ ] User can exit with complete workflow done
close_reason: Updated INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md with streamlined end-of-interview flow: Q&A summary display, approval prompts via AskUserQuestion, inline PRD generation and display, PRD approval, task creation, and session completion instructions.

title: Unify loops and rename lisa functions to generic terms
description:
## Epic

Consolidate lisa.sh and ralph.sh into a single unified ralph.sh automation loop, and rename all lisa-specific references to generic terms like 'idea refinement', 'interview', 'PRD generation'.

## Current State

Two separate loops with persona-specific naming:
- **lisa.sh**: Watches for features, generates interviews, creates PRDs, handles approval
- **ralph.sh**: Implements approved tasks from beads

## Desired State

Single unified **ralph.sh** that:
- Uses one while loop with configurable poll interval
- Handles idea refinement (interviews, PRD generation, approval)
- Handles task implementation
- No references to 'lisa' - use generic functional names
- Shared logging infrastructure
- Single process to manage

## Naming Changes

| Old (lisa-specific) | New (generic) |
|---------------------|---------------|
| lisa.sh | (removed, merged into ralph.sh) |
| assignee=lisa | assignee=ralph (or remove assignee check) |
| 'lisa' in prompts | 'idea refinement' / 'interview' |
| handle_lisa_work() | handle_refinement_work() |
| LISA_PROMPT | INTERVIEW_PROMPT / REFINEMENT_PROMPT |

## Architecture

```
ralph.sh
├── Main while loop
│   ├── check_for_refinement_work()
│   │   ├── handle_new_features()      # Generate interviews
│   │   ├── handle_interviewed()       # Generate PRDs  
│   │   └── handle_approved()          # Create implementation tasks
│   └── check_for_implementation_work()
│       └── implement_tasks()          # Execute implementation
├── Shared utilities
│   ├── logging
│   ├── polling
│   └── process management
└── Configuration
    ├── POLL_INTERVAL
    ├── IDLE_SLEEP
    └── LOG_DIR
```

## Files to Update
- template/lisa.sh → merge into template/ralph.sh, delete
- template/prompts/* → rename lisa references
- template/CLAUDE.md.jinja → update workflow docs
- template/interview.sh → update assignee references
- template/idea.sh → change assignee from lisa to ralph
- copier.yaml → update post-copy message
- README.md → update documentation
- tests/test-lisa.sh → rename to test-ralph.sh, update

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Single ralph.sh handles full workflow (refinement + implementation)
- [ ] No references to 'lisa' anywhere in template
- [ ] Generic functional naming throughout
- [ ] All existing functionality preserved
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] Tests updated and pass
close_reason: Unified lisa.sh and ralph.sh into single ralph.sh with --refinement-only and --implementation-only flags. All lisa references removed. Tests and docs updated.

title: Fix interview.sh: pipe prompt to claude stdin for AskUserQuestion
description:
## Task

Update interview.sh to pipe the prompt to Claude via stdin instead of using flags. This is required for AskUserQuestion to work properly.

## Current (broken)
```bash
claude --some-flag "prompt here"
# Claude opens but doesn't execute the prompt
```

## Correct Pattern
```bash
echo "Your prompt instructing Claude to use AskUserQuestion..." | claude
```

## Implementation

Update interview.sh to:
```bash
# Build the prompt with feature context
PROMPT="You are conducting an interview for feature $FEATURE_ID: $FEATURE_TITLE

Read the feature details and use AskUserQuestion to ask the user clarifying questions.
Start immediately by asking your first question.

[Include INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md content here]"

# Pipe to claude
echo "$PROMPT" | claude
```

## Why This Works
- Piping to stdin causes Claude to immediately process the prompt
- Claude will execute tool calls (like AskUserQuestion) as instructed
- No manual user input required to start

## Files to Modify
- `template/interview.sh` - Change claude invocation to use pipe

## Related Issues
- ortus-4ms (the bug this fixes)
- ortus-223 (earlier context issue)
- ortus-kfr (original fix attempt)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] interview.sh pipes prompt to claude via stdin
- [ ] Claude immediately starts asking questions via AskUserQuestion
- [ ] Full interview flow works end-to-end
close_reason: Updated interview.sh to pipe prompt to stdin. Test passes.

title: Fix interview.sh: Claude not using AskUserQuestion for interview
description:
## Problem

interview.sh launches Claude but Claude just sits at the prompt instead of using AskUserQuestion to conduct the interview. The INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md instructions are not being followed.

## Current Behavior
1. idea.sh works - prompts for idea, expands it, creates feature
2. interview.sh finds the feature correctly (e.g., calc-83a 'tiktaktoe in the cli')
3. Claude Code opens
4. Claude sits at prompt waiting for user input
5. No AskUserQuestion is triggered

## Expected Behavior
1. interview.sh finds feature
2. Claude Code opens with context
3. Claude IMMEDIATELY uses AskUserQuestion to ask first interview question
4. Interview proceeds as back-and-forth conversation
5. Answers written to beads as comments

## Likely Root Cause
The prompt/context passed to Claude is not instructing it to immediately start asking questions. The INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md may not be:
- Being passed to Claude at all
- Missing explicit instruction to 'immediately ask the first question'
- Not triggering Claude's AskUserQuestion tool

## Debug Steps
1. Check how interview.sh invokes claude CLI
2. Verify INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md content is passed
3. Add explicit 'Start now by asking your first question' instruction

## Related Issues
- ortus-kfr (original fix attempt)
- ortus-223 (context not piped)

## Files to Fix
- `template/interview.sh` - Verify prompt is passed correctly
- `template/prompts/INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md` - Add explicit 'start immediately' instruction

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Claude immediately uses AskUserQuestion upon session start
- [ ] No user prompt required to begin interview
- [ ] Full interview flow works end-to-end

title: Fix interview.sh: Claude not receiving context, doesn't start asking questions
description:
## Problem

interview.sh finds the feature correctly and launches Claude, but Claude just sits at the prompt without asking any questions. The context (feature ID, description, INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md instructions) is not being piped into the Claude session.

## Current Behavior
1. interview.sh finds feature (e.g., calc-ixr 'tiktaktoe cli game')
2. Claude Code opens
3. Claude does nothing - waits for user input instead of starting interview

## Expected Behavior
1. interview.sh finds feature
2. Claude Code opens WITH the feature context and interview instructions
3. Claude immediately starts asking questions via AskUserQuestion

The claude CLI is likely being invoked without passing the initial prompt/context. Need to use `claude --print` or pass the prompt as an argument.

title: Fix interview.sh: no initial prompt and missing exit prompt
description:
## Problem

interview.sh has two issues:

1. **No initial prompt**: When the interview starts, it just opens Claude without priming the session. Claude doesn't know what to do or which feature to interview about.

2. **No exit prompt**: When the interview completes, the user is left in the Claude session with no indication they should exit. Should prompt user to exit Claude when done.

## Expected Behavior

1. interview.sh should pass the feature context and INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md instructions to Claude so it immediately starts asking questions
2. After writing all answers to beads and adding the 'interviewed' label, Claude should tell the user the interview is complete and prompt them to exit (or auto-exit)

- `template/prompts/INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md` - Add instruction to prompt user to exit when done

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Claude starts with context about which feature to interview
- [ ] Claude immediately begins asking questions (no blank session)
- [ ] After interview completion, user is prompted to exit Claude

title: Add interview.sh for interactive Claude-powered interviews
description:
## Problem
Lisa's current workflow requires users to answer interview questions by navigating beads, adding comments, and closing them. This is clunky and breaks conversational flow.

## Solution
Create interview.sh — an interactive Claude Code session that conducts dynamic interviews using AskUserQuestion.

## Design

### Flow
1. `./interview.sh` auto-detects features assigned to lisa that need interviewing
2. Claude conducts a dynamic interview using `AskUserQuestion` — asking follow-ups based on answers
3. Answers are written back to beads as comments (for audit trail)
4. Adds 'interviewed' label to the feature
5. Lisa's loop detects the label and generates PRD

### Files to Create
- `template/interview.sh` — Bash wrapper that invokes `claude` CLI
- `template/prompts/INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md` — Claude's instructions for conducting interviews

### interview.sh Behavior
- Auto-detect: Find features where assignee=lisa and no 'interviewed' label
- If multiple found, let user choose which to interview
- Run `claude` with INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md
- Pass feature ID and description to Claude

### INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md Instructions
- Read the feature details from beads
- Ask 5-8 targeted questions about requirements, scope, constraints
- Use AskUserQuestion for each question (dynamic follow-ups allowed)
- Write each answer as a comment on the feature bead
- When complete, add 'interviewed' label to trigger Lisa's PRD generation

### Integration with Lisa
- Lisa should check for 'interviewed' label instead of closed question beads
- When 'interviewed' label found, Lisa generates PRD from feature + comments
- Remove the old question-bead generation logic from Lisa

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] interview.sh auto-detects pending features
- [ ] Claude asks dynamic questions via AskUserQuestion
- [ ] Answers saved to beads as comments
- [ ] 'interviewed' label added on completion
- [ ] Lisa generates PRD from interviewed features
- [ ] Old question-bead logic removed from lisa.sh

title: Epic: Unify PRD pipeline into lisa.sh
description:
## Summary

Replace the fragmented PRD generation scripts with a single unified loop script called `lisa.sh`. Lisa handles the full idea-to-implementation pipeline: receiving ideas, conducting interviews via beads, generating PRDs, and handing off to ralph.

## Current State (to be removed)

- generate-prd.sh - Interactive PRD generation
- generate-interview.sh - Creates interview question beads
- collect-interview.sh - Collects answered questions
- generate-prd-from-interview.sh - Generates PRD from answers
- prd-pipeline.sh - Orchestrates the async workflow

## New Architecture

### lisa.sh - The unified PRD loop

**Execution model**: Continuous loop (like current prd-pipeline.sh)
- Runs in background: `./lisa.sh`
- Polls for ideas assigned to 'lisa'
- Processes ideas through interview → PRD → handoff stages

**Workflow**:
1. User submits idea: `bd create --title="My idea" --type=idea --assignee=lisa`
2. Lisa generates interview questions as child beads (blocking dependencies on the idea)
3. Human answers by commenting on question beads, then closes them
4. When all questions closed, Lisa generates PRD document (prd/PRD-<name>.md)
5. Human reviews PRD, adds 'approved' label when ready
6. Lisa creates implementation tasks with --assignee=ralph
7. Ralph picks up tasks and implements

**State tracking via labels**:
- `prd:interviewing` - Interview questions created, waiting for answers
- `prd:ready` - Interview complete, PRD generated, awaiting approval
- `prd:approved` - Human approved, tasks being created for ralph
- (idea closed after handoff complete)

## Files to Delete

- template/generate-prd.sh
- template/generate-interview.sh  
- template/collect-interview.sh
- template/generate-prd-from-interview.sh
- template/prd-pipeline.sh

## Files to Create/Update

- template/lisa.sh (new unified script)
- copier.yaml (_tasks and _message_after_copy)
- template/prd/PRD-PROMPT.md (may need updates)
- template/prd/PRD-INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md (may need updates or removal)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] lisa.sh runs as continuous loop processing ideas assigned to 'lisa'
- [ ] Interview questions created as blocking child beads
- [ ] Human answers via comments, closes question beads when done
- [ ] PRD document generated at prd/PRD-<name>.md
- [ ] Human approval via 'approved' label triggers task creation
- [ ] Tasks auto-assigned to ralph
- [ ] Old scripts removed from template/
- [ ] copier.yaml updated (chmod, messages)
- [ ] Documentation updated

title: Epic: Merge ralph.sh and mega-ralph.sh with task limit option
description:
## Summary

Consolidate ralph.sh and mega-ralph.sh into a single script. By default, ralph runs in 'mega' mode (completing all tasks until none remain). A new --tasks N option limits ralph to completing only N tasks.

## Behavior

- `./ralph.sh` - Run until all tasks complete (current mega-ralph behavior)
- `./ralph.sh --tasks 1` - Complete exactly 1 task then exit
- `./ralph.sh --tasks 5` - Complete up to 5 tasks then exit

## Changes Required

1. Merge mega-ralph.sh logic into ralph.sh
2. Add --tasks N option parsing
3. Remove mega-ralph.sh from template/
4. Update copier.yaml _tasks to remove mega-ralph.sh from chmod
5. Update any documentation referencing mega-ralph.sh

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ralph.sh runs all tasks by default (mega mode)
- [ ] ralph.sh --tasks N completes exactly N tasks
- [ ] mega-ralph.sh removed from template
- [ ] Documentation updated
close_reason: Merged mega-ralph.sh into ralph.sh with --tasks N option. Default behavior now runs until queue empty. Added --iterations and --idle-sleep options.

title: Implement PRD pipeline with async human Q&A via subtasks
description:
Create an automated PRD pipeline that:

1. **Idea Intake**: Picks up ideas assigned to 'prd-pipeline' via `bd ready --assignee prd-pipeline`

2. **Async Q&A via Subtasks**: Instead of interactive interviews, the pipeline:
   - Reads the idea description
   - Uses prd/PRD-PROMPT.md to generate discovery questions
   - Creates subtasks for each question (assigned to a human)
   - Human answers questions by updating the subtask description or adding comments
   - Human closes subtask when answered
   - Pipeline waits for all Q&A subtasks to close (uses dependency blocking)

3. **PRD Generation**: Once all Q&A subtasks are closed:
   - Collects answers from subtasks
   - Generates PRD document to prd/PRD-[name].md using prd/PRD-PROMPT.md
   - Explodes PRD into implementation tasks/epics assigned to 'ralph'
   - Closes the original idea

**Scripts needed**:
- `prd-pipeline.sh` - Loop that runs `bd ready --assignee prd-pipeline`

**Workflow**:
```
Human creates idea (assignee: prd-pipeline)
    ↓
prd-pipeline.sh picks it up
    ↓
Creates Q&A subtasks (assignee: human, blocks: idea)
    ↓
Human answers async, closes subtasks
    ↓
Pipeline detects all Q&A done
    ↓
Generates PRD using prd/PRD-PROMPT.md, explodes to tasks (assignee: ralph)
    ↓
ralph.sh picks up implementation work
```
close_reason: Done: Implemented full async PRD pipeline with generate-qa.sh, collect-qa.sh, generate-prd-from-qa.sh, and prd-pipeline.sh

title: Decision: should smoke test be skippable via env var (ORTUS_SKIP_SANDBOX_CHECK=1)?
description: Open question from PRD: Should the Phase 1 smoke test be skippable via env var (ORTUS_SKIP_SANDBOX_CHECK=1) for users running Ortus on intentionally unsandboxed CI runners, or is that an anti-pattern we should NOT enable? The argument against: skippability re-introduces the silent-degradation failure mode this PRD is designed to eliminate. The argument for: legitimate CI environments may run with their own isolation (Docker, ephemeral VMs) where the host sandbox is redundant or unavailable. Decision must land before or alongside T1.4 (smoke test implementation).
design: Author lean: anti-pattern-disallow. The PRD's stated success metric is 'zero Ralph iterations execute unsandboxed without an explicit operator opt-out' — an env var IS an explicit opt-out and would satisfy the metric, but it also re-creates the silent-failure surface this PRD eliminates. The Tier 2 (--docker) escape hatch already covers the 'unsandboxed CI' use case better than env-var-skip would. Recommend disallow with a comment in ralph.sh explaining why.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
1. A decision is recorded (skip-allowed vs. anti-pattern-disallow).
2. If 'skip-allowed', a follow-up implementation task is filed to add the env-var check to ralph.sh, with explicit acceptance that the env var must be loud (banner-style log line) when active.
3. If 'anti-pattern-disallow', the decision is recorded with rationale so a future contributor doesn't silently re-introduce skippability.

Testing:
- Decision recorded in this issue's notes with rationale.
- If implemented (skip-allowed), unset ORTUS_SKIP_SANDBOX_CHECK and run ralph.sh on a no-bubblewrap host → fails fast (T1.4 baseline). Then export ORTUS_SKIP_SANDBOX_CHECK=1 and re-run → smoke test skipped, with a loud warning banner in stderr/stdout. Verify the warning is present.
- If 'disallow', no follow-up task; decision is the artifact.
notes:
**Decision: anti-pattern-disallow**

The Phase 1 smoke test SHALL NOT be skippable via env var (e.g., ORTUS_SKIP_SANDBOX_CHECK=1).

**Rationale:**

1. **Re-introduces the failure mode the PRD eliminates.** The whole point of Phase 1 is to close the silent-degradation hole where sandbox.enabled: true silently became unsandboxed execution. Adding a skip flag rebuilds the same hole one layer up: a CI runner with ORTUS_SKIP_SANDBOX_CHECK=1 baked into its environment would behave indistinguishably from a host where the sandbox silently failed. Even with a 'loud banner', a banner that scrolls past in CI logs is functionally silent.

2. **Tier 2 (--docker) is the correct escape hatch for legitimate unsandboxed-host scenarios.** The PRD's two-tier model already covers 'host where native sandbox is unavailable or redundant': run with --docker, which provides outer isolation via Docker's own sandbox. That path is explicit (operator passes --docker on the command line every invocation), discoverable (it's in --help and README), and structurally distinct from the default. An env-var skip is none of those — it's invisible at invocation, persists across runs, and looks identical to the safe path.

3. **The PRD success metric ('zero Ralph iterations execute unsandboxed without an explicit operator opt-out') is satisfied without env-var skip.** --docker IS the explicit operator opt-out. Adding a second opt-out path doesn't improve the metric, just dilutes it.

4. **No follow-up task required.** Per AC#3, recording the decision with rationale is the artifact. The decision is durable: future contributors reading this issue will see why skippability was rejected.

**Operational consequence for T1.4 (ortus-5x7j.4):** Smoke test should NOT check for any skip env var. Add a brief inline comment in template/ortus/ralph.sh near the smoke test pointing to this decision (ortus-hhq9) so a future contributor doesn't silently re-introduce skippability.

**Note on --docker discoverability:** Phase 2 (ortus-lfft) is responsible for ensuring --docker is discoverable from ralph.sh --help and README. If a host operator is told 'sandbox unavailable, install bubblewrap' but they're on a host where bubblewrap genuinely cannot run (e.g., locked-down CI), the smoke-test failure message should hint at --docker as the alternative path. That's a Phase 2 / Phase 1 polish item, not a reason to add env-var skip here.
close_reason: Decision recorded: anti-pattern-disallow. Smoke test SHALL NOT be skippable via env var; --docker (Tier 2) is the correct escape hatch. Rationale + operational consequence for T1.4 (ortus-5x7j.4) documented in notes. No follow-up task per AC#3.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- Recorded decision in issue notes: anti-pattern-disallow (no env-var skip for smoke test)
- Documented 4-point rationale + operational consequence for ortus-5x7j.4 (T1.4)
- No follow-up task filed (per AC#3: decision is the artifact)

**Verification**: bd show ortus-hhq9 confirms NOTES section contains verdict + rationale; AC#1 and AC#3 satisfied; AC#2 N/A (skip-allowed branch not taken).

title: Decision: surface clear error if --docker is used without Docker installed
description: Open question from PRD: How should Ortus surface a clear error if a user runs './ortus/ralph.sh --docker' without Docker installed? Two options: (1) detect-and-message — ralph.sh checks for 'command -v docker' and 'docker sandbox --help' before invoking and emits a friendly install hint on failure; (2) let 'docker sandbox run' fail loudly — user sees Docker's native error. Decision must land before T2.2 acceptance to set the implementation expectation.
design: Recommendation: detect-and-message, mirroring the T1.4 pattern. Friendly errors are the Ortus tone ('git push not user-hostile'). Minimal cost: a 'command -v docker || die hint' line at the top of the --docker branch in ralph.sh.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
1. A decision is recorded (detect-and-message vs. let-docker-fail).
2. If 'detect-and-message', a follow-up implementation task is filed under EP2 with acceptance criteria.
3. The decision is consistent with the Phase 1 smoke-test pattern (T1.4) — i.e., if Phase 1 emits friendly OS-specific messages on bubblewrap absence, Phase 2 should match for consistency.

Testing:
- Decision recorded in this issue's notes with rationale.
- If 'detect-and-message', verify the follow-up task exists via 'bd show <follow-up-id>' and dependency edge to T2.2.
notes:
**Decision: detect-and-message** (Option 1).

**Rationale:**
- Matches T1.4 (ortus-5x7j.4) Phase 1 smoke-test pattern: 'command -v bwrap' / 'command -v sandbox-exec' precondition with friendly OS-specific install hint, exits non-zero on absence.
- Consistent Ortus tone: friendly errors with remediation steps rather than raw Docker error messages.
- Minimal cost: one 'command -v docker || die hint' line at the top of the --docker branch in ralph.sh.

**Implementation contract for follow-up:**
- ralph.sh, when --docker is set, runs 'command -v docker' as a precondition.
- On absence, log 'ERROR: --docker requires Docker' with platform-aware install hints (e.g., 'macOS: brew install docker / Docker Desktop; Linux: see https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/').
- Also probe 'docker sandbox --help' to detect the bundled-image subcommand; emit a separate hint pointing to Docker Desktop / docker-sandbox availability if absent.
- Exits non-zero (≥1) before the Ralph iteration loop starts.

**Reference:** template/ortus/ralph.sh:56-83 (sandbox_smoke_test) is the canonical pattern to mirror.
close_reason: Decision: detect-and-message (Option 1), mirrors T1.4 friendly-error pattern. Follow-up implementation task ortus-lfft.7 filed under EP2.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- ortus-heot notes — recorded decision: detect-and-message (Option 1), with rationale and implementation contract
- ortus-lfft.7 created — follow-up implementation task under EP2 (parent: ortus-lfft) with full acceptance criteria for the 'command -v docker' + 'docker sandbox --help' precondition checks in ralph.sh's --docker branch

**Verification**: AC#1 met (decision in notes). AC#2 met (ortus-lfft.7 exists with acceptance criteria, parented to EP2 ortus-lfft). AC#3 met (decision mirrors T1.4 pattern at template/ortus/ralph.sh:56-83 — 'command -v bwrap' / 'command -v sandbox-exec' with friendly install hints, exit non-zero). Dep edge to T2.2 deferred — T2.2 does not yet exist as a bd issue; ortus-lfft.7's notes flag the wiring for whenever T2.2 lands.

title: Decision: --docker mode toolchain installation strategy
description: Open question from PRD: Should --docker mode also try to install the project's language toolchains inside the container, or rely on 'docker sandbox run claude' bundled image (which already includes Node, Python, Go)? Recommendation pending analysis of bundled image contents and version freshness. Decision must land before or alongside T2.2 (Docker invocation branch) so the implementation stays consistent.
design: The PRD's Tier 2 design relies on the bundled docker-sandbox-claude image to avoid Dockerfile maintenance (NFR-003). Lean toward 'use bundled' unless version-staleness analysis shows a blocker. If a project's language toolchain version differs significantly from the bundled image, document that as a known limitation rather than expanding scope.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
1. A decision is recorded in this issue (use bundled image vs. per-project toolchain install).
2. Decision is linked to relevant Phase 2 tasks (T2.2, T2.3) so they implement consistently.
3. If 'install per-project', a follow-up implementation task is filed under EP2.

Testing:
- Decision documented in this issue's notes/design field with rationale.
- If 'use bundled', confirm Node/Python/Go versions are present in the bundled image: docker sandbox run claude -- node --version; same for python --version and go version. Capture version numbers in the decision.
- If 'install per-project', verify the follow-up task exists via 'bd show <follow-up-id>' and dependency edge to T2.2.
notes:
**Decision: Use the bundled `docker/sandbox-templates:claude-code` image** (no per-project toolchain install).

**Rationale:**
- Aligns with NFR-003 (no Dockerfile in the repo or generated project) and the PRD's Tier 2 design.
- Bundled image already covers the PRD's stated language set (Node, Python, Go).
- Avoids Dockerfile maintenance and image-rebuild churn; upstream Docker maintains the image.
- Per-project install would expand scope (toolchain selection logic, version pinning, build-time install steps) without addressing the actual Tier 2 motivation (container-level isolation for unattended overnight runs).
- Rust / Ruby projects are a documented limitation, not a blocker — Tier 1 (host claude) remains supported for those stacks.

**Version evidence (captured 2026-04-28 from `docker/sandbox-templates:claude-code` digest sha256:71baeadc39d7646d51e10ac11c4784b4043a2727f0bcc707c9277acca5b1cd47):**
- Node: v20.19.4
- Python: 3.13.7
- Go: 1.24.4
- Java (OpenJDK): 21.0.10
- Git: 2.51.0
- Claude Code: 2.1.121
- Rust: MISSING
- Ruby: MISSING

Probe command (reproducible): `docker run --rm docker/sandbox-templates:claude-code bash -lc 'node --version; python3 --version; go version; java --version; git --version; claude --version'`

`docker sandbox` plugin version observed: github.com/docker/sandboxes/cli-plugin v0.6.0 (Docker 29.3.1).

**Implementation contract for downstream tasks:**
- ortus-lfft.2 (T2.2): invoke `docker sandbox run claude --name ortus-ralph -- <args>` with the default bundled image (no `--template` override). No Dockerfile is added to the repo or generated project.
- ortus-lfft.3 (T2.3): forward --fast / --tasks N / --iterations N to the in-container claude argv array; no toolchain-bootstrap step needed inside the container.
- ortus-lfft.4 / ortus-lfft.5 (README): document the supported toolchains (Node/Python/Go/Java) and call out Rust/Ruby as a known limitation in --docker mode — for those stacks, recommend Tier 1 (no --docker).

**Known limitation to document in README (FR-008 / ortus-lfft.4):**
Projects using Rust or Ruby cannot run `--docker` mode against the bundled image. Use Tier 1 (no --docker) for those stacks, or wait for Phase-3 work that may reconsider per-project toolchain install if user demand emerges.

**Cross-references:**
- Closing-direction issue: ortus-lfft (Phase 2 epic).
- Direct downstream consumers: ortus-lfft.2, ortus-lfft.3, ortus-lfft.4, ortus-lfft.5.
- Companion decision: ortus-heot (detect-and-message for missing Docker, closed).
close_reason: Decision: use bundled docker/sandbox-templates:claude-code image (no per-project toolchain install). Aligns with NFR-003; bundled image confirmed to contain Node 20.19.4 / Python 3.13.7 / Go 1.24.4. Rust/Ruby documented as --docker-mode limitation. Unblocks ortus-lfft.2 → .3/.4/.5/.6.

title: README — enableWeakerNestedSandbox under Tier 2 section
description: Document enableWeakerNestedSandbox: true in the README's Tier 2 section. Explain that running the native sandbox inside a Docker container on Linux requires this flag because nested bubblewrap has reduced isolation guarantees (the container is the strong boundary; the inner sandbox adds defense-in-depth without claiming full nested isolation). Implements FR-010.
design: Place under the Tier 2 heading, after the Tier 1 vs Tier 2 decision table (T2.5) and the bind-mount/auth notes (T2.4). Keep it concise: 2-3 sentences explaining the flag, the Linux nesting issue, and why the trade-off is acceptable. No need to repeat full Tier 2 setup instructions.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
1. README's Tier 2 section mentions enableWeakerNestedSandbox: true.
2. Section explains the rationale (nested bubblewrap on Linux, container is the primary boundary, inner sandbox is defense-in-depth).
3. Section explicitly states the security trade-off is acceptable in this configuration.
4. Section reaches a generated project after copier copy.

Testing:

- Visual read of the section confirms the rationale (not just the flag name) is articulated.
- copier copy --defaults template /tmp/test-fr010; rg 'enableWeakerNestedSandbox' /tmp/test-fr010/README.md → match.

title: Template settings.json with per-language allowedDomains (incl. polyglot)
description: Convert template/.claude/settings.json (or rename to settings.json.jinja) to a Jinja-templated file that injects a different allowedDomains list based on language_profile. Profiles: python → PyPI (pypi.org, files.pythonhosted.org); javascript → npm (registry.npmjs.org); go → Go module proxy (proxy.golang.org, sum.golang.org); rust → crates.io (crates.io, static.crates.io); polyglot → all four. Always include Anthropic API and GitHub regardless of profile. Implements remainder of FR-009.
design: Use Jinja conditionals around the registry-specific entries. Always-on domains (Anthropic, GitHub) live outside the conditional. Keep the union list (polyglot) as the canonical reference; single-language profiles are subsets. Test JSON validity in each profile because Jinja can produce trailing-comma issues — guard with a final trailing entry pattern or careful concatenation.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
1. template/.claude/settings.json (or .jinja variant) renders different allowedDomains based on language_profile.
2. All five profiles (python, javascript, go, rust, polyglot) produce valid JSON.
3. Anthropic API (api.anthropic.com) and GitHub domains appear in every profile.
4. Polyglot profile is a strict superset of any single-language profile.

Testing:
- copier copy --data language_profile=python template /tmp/test-py-l → 'jq .sandbox.network.allowedDomains' contains pypi.org and files.pythonhosted.org but NOT registry.npmjs.org, static.crates.io, or proxy.golang.org.
- Repeat for javascript, go, rust profiles — each shows only its registry plus Anthropic+GitHub.
- copier copy --data language_profile=polyglot ... → all four registries present.
- jq empty on each generated settings.json → exit 0 (valid JSON).
- Diff polyglot vs single-language: polyglot must be a superset (set-difference returns empty for each single-language profile against polyglot).
