design: Use copier's choices: list for the enum. help: text should explain what each profile controls (allowedDomains list size). Default polyglot keeps backward compatibility and avoids surprising existing users on copier update (NFR-004 surface).

title: NFR-104 cumulative overrun: prd-decompose-prompt.md grew 37 lines vs pre-PRD baseline (budget 25)
description:
ortus-io5h.3's literal acceptance test (`git diff codegraph -- file | grep -c '^+'`) returns 0 because HEAD is on the codegraph branch itself, so it passes trivially. However, cumulative net additions to ortus/prompts/prd-decompose-prompt.md vs the pre-PRD baseline commit ef14d52 (last commit touching the file before FR-301..304 work began) are 37 lines, which exceeds NFR-104's 25-line readability budget by 12 lines.

Per-commit breakdown:
- c8c4ca0 (ortus-4x1n.1, FR-301+FR-302): +19/-0
- 4fa3002 (ortus-4x1n.2, FR-303 annotation): +10/-0
- 4287144 (ortus-4x1n.3, FR-304 Likely files): +8/-0
- Total: +37 net additions (no deletions in any commit).

Per-commit close reasons each noted their delta as 'well under' budget, but cumulative drift was not caught because each delta was checked against the immediate prior commit, not the pre-PRD baseline.

Two viable fix paths:
1. Trim the FR-301..304 block by ~12 lines (consolidate redundant prose, shorten Appendix F template explanation).
2. Reaffirm or revise NFR-104's 25-line budget for prd-decompose-prompt.md (PRD owner decision).

Note the same methodology gap likely affects ortus-io5h.1 (ralph-prompt.md ≤80 line budget) — verify cumulative there too.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
- git diff ef14d52 -- ortus/prompts/prd-decompose-prompt.md | grep '^+' | grep -v '^+++' | wc -l <= 25.

title: Ralph prompt: skip push step when no git remote is configured (ZFC-aligned state check)
description:
Ralph step 9 (Commit & Push) unconditionally runs 'git pull --rebase --autostash && bd dolt push && git push'. For projects without a git remote (fresh copier generation, solo experimentation), this silently fails or stalls: the model improvises (e.g. 'git remote -v' to check, then skips push) instead of following the prompt. Evidence: /tmp/ortus-branch-test/logs/ralph-20260420-211116.log — 16 iterations, 9 commits made, zero 'git push' / 'bd dolt push' calls. The LLM correctly inferred the right behavior, but the prompt should encode it so every loop handles it identically.

**ZFC alignment (per ZFC.md):**
This is a state check, not a judgment. 'Does a git remote exist?' is pure IO — exactly the 'Pure IO: reading files, calling APIs, writing state' row of the allowed column. Not keyword routing, not heuristic ranking, not semantic analysis of unstructured input. A shell-level conditional on 'git remote' output is the correct shape. Delegating this to a model call would be overkill (no judgment required) and would violate the 'dumb pipes' principle in the opposite direction.

**Specific change — step 9 of ralph-prompt.md:**

Current:

    9. **Commit & Push**: Stage, commit with issue ID in message, then Already up to date.
Pushing to Dolt remote...
Push complete.

Proposed:

    9. **Commit & Push**: Stage, commit with issue ID in message, then run:

           if [ -n "$(git remote)" ]; then
             git pull --rebase --autostash && bd dolt push && git push
           else
             echo "No git remote configured; skipping push (local-only project)."
           fi

Keep this shape — do NOT expand into a decision tree on project type, labels, or issue shape. The only input is the boolean 'does a remote exist'.

**Locations to update (parity critical):**
- ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md — step 9
- template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja — mirror exactly
- AGENTS.md session-close block — the 'PUSH TO REMOTE — This is MANDATORY' section needs softening: 'when a remote is configured' added to the rule; the bash snippet wrapped in the same if/else
- template/AGENTS.md — mirror
- CLAUDE.md 'Session Completion' — same softening on 'Work is NOT complete until git push succeeds'; reword to '...when a remote is configured'
- template/CLAUDE.md.jinja if it has the same block — mirror

**Non-goals:**
- Do not add any heuristics ('if project is a test scaffold, skip push' — that would be semantic inference, ZFC violation).
- Do not change the session-close protocol for projects that DO have a remote — push remains mandatory there.
acceptance_criteria:
1. ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md and template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja both contain the if/else guarded push block, byte-identical.
2. AGENTS.md, template/AGENTS.md, and CLAUDE.md (+ template if applicable) are updated consistently — push is mandatory only when a remote is configured.
3. 'make parity' exits 0.
4. Smoke test in a no-remote repo: a Ralph iteration completes, emits the 'No git remote configured; skipping push' message, and exits with COMPLETE.
5. Smoke test in a with-remote repo: a Ralph iteration completes with a successful push (regression check — normal workflow unchanged).
6. ZFC.md rubric rows are satisfied — reviewer confirms the change is a state check, not a heuristic or decision tree.
close_reason: ZFC-aligned push guard: all 5 files updated, ralph-prompt byte-identical, parity OK, no-remote and with-remote smoke tests pass

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md — step 9 wraps push in 'if [ -n "$(git remote)" ]' guard
- template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja — byte-identical mirror of step 9 change
- AGENTS.md — Session Completion block: heading/rules softened to 'when a remote is configured'; bash snippet wrapped in if/else
- template/AGENTS.md — mirror of AGENTS.md change
- CLAUDE.md — Session Completion block in BEADS INTEGRATION section: same softening + if/else wrap
- template/CLAUDE.md.jinja — unchanged (no session-completion block present, confirmed via grep)

**Verification**: make parity OK; diff of step 9 across the two ralph-prompt files confirms byte-identical; bash smoke test passes in both a no-remote temp repo (prints 'No git remote configured; skipping push (local-only project).') and a with-remote temp repo (enters push branch). ZFC rubric: change is pure shell-level state check on 'git remote' output — no keyword routing, no heuristics, no decision tree on issue shape.

title: Update README: upstream prereqs, document bd dolt push, add make parity
description: README.md currently only lists copier under top-level Prerequisites, but contributors working in the Ortus repo itself also need beads v1.0.0+, dolt, and claude to run ortus/idea.sh, ortus/interview.sh, or ortus/ralph.sh. The v1.0.0+ requirement is documented further down under 'Requirements' (generated projects), but that section is implicitly targeted at end-users of the template — not the Ortus contributor who just cloned this repo. Additionally, the v1.0.0 migration (bd sync → bd dolt push, embedded → server mode) landed in this branch and the README does not yet mention the correct remote-sync command for contributors. Finally, 'make parity' was added in ortus-oxx and is not documented.
design:
Keep scope tight: README only. Do NOT duplicate content from AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md — link or cross-reference them instead.

Specific edits:

1. Upstream the dolt/beads/claude/jq/rg/fd prereqs into the top 'Prerequisites' section (README.md:9-15). Keep copier at the top since it's what you run first (copier copy gh:who/ortus), but add a paragraph or sub-list clarifying that once you start using idea.sh/ralph.sh (either in the template repo or a generated project), the full Requirements table applies. Simplest form: a one-liner under Prerequisites that says 'To run any ortus/*.sh script (locally or in a generated project), also install the tools under Requirements below' with a link to #requirements.

2. Add a one-liner under the 'beads v1.0.0+ required' subsection (or a new 'Workflow' subsection) documenting 'bd dolt push' as the correct remote-sync command. Phrasing: 'Remote sync uses bd dolt push / bd dolt pull. The v0.55-era bd sync command was removed in v1.0.0.' Cross-reference AGENTS.md for the full session-close workflow rather than duplicating it.

3. Add 'make parity' to a 'Development' or 'Contributing' subsection (new, short). Should read something like: 'Run make parity before committing changes to ortus/ or template/ortus/ — it detects drift between the canonical tree and the Jinja mirror.'

Do NOT:
- Remove or rewrite the existing 'beads v1.0.0+ required' paragraph; it is correct as written.
- Change the Requirements table structure; add to it only if a new tool is missing.
- Add language-specific content.
- Introduce emoji or marketing-style prose.
acceptance_criteria:
1. README.md top 'Prerequisites' section references (or links to) the full tool requirements so a contributor knows dolt/beads/claude are needed to run ortus scripts, not just copier.

2. README.md documents 'bd dolt push' as the correct remote-sync command somewhere in the requirements area, with a clear note that 'bd sync' was removed in beads v1.0.0.

3. README.md mentions 'make parity' and what it does (drift check between ortus/ and template/ortus/).

4. Verification: (a) rg 'bd sync' README.md returns no hits that reference it as a live command (historical/migration context is fine if clearly framed); (b) rg 'make parity' README.md returns at least one hit; (c) rg 'bd dolt push' README.md returns at least one hit; (d) rg 'dolt' in the top Prerequisites section (lines 1-20) returns a hit or a link to the section that does.

5. No content duplication from AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md — cross-references only.

6. 'copier copy --defaults template /tmp/readme-smoke' still generates cleanly (README.md is not templated so this is a regression check, not a functional test).
close_reason: README now upstreams ortus-script prereqs, documents bd dolt push, and documents make parity; no content duplicated from AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md

- README.md beads v1.0.0+ subsection — added one-liner documenting 'bd dolt push' / 'bd dolt pull' as remote-sync commands and that 'bd sync' was removed in v1.0.0; cross-referenced AGENTS.md for session-close workflow
- README.md — added new Development subsection documenting 'make parity' as the drift check between ortus/ and template/ortus/

title: Move ZFC.md and LABELS.md from ortus/prompts/ to repo root; drop from template tree
description:
The ortus/prompts/ folder is meant exclusively for prompts an agent consumes at runtime. ZFC.md and LABELS.md are contributor-facing reference docs (a rubric for writing ZFC-aligned prompts, and a state-machine label vocabulary doc). Nothing in the codebase reads them programmatically — confirmed by grep: no script, prompt, CLAUDE.md, or AGENTS.md references them outside the PRD and the two files cross-linking each other.

They are also Ortus-development docs, not runtime material a generated downstream project needs. Drop them from the template tree entirely — downstream projects inherit prompts that already comply with the rubric, not the rubric itself.

**File moves:**
- ortus/prompts/ZFC.md → ZFC.md
- ortus/prompts/LABELS.md → LABELS.md
- Delete template/ortus/prompts/ZFC.md
- Delete template/ortus/prompts/LABELS.md

Use 'git mv' for the canonical moves to preserve history.

**Link updates (relative paths change when files move up two directories):**
- ZFC.md line 3: '../../prd/PRD-zero-framework-cognition.md' → 'prd/PRD-zero-framework-cognition.md'
- LABELS.md line 3: '../../prd/PRD-zero-framework-cognition.md' → 'prd/PRD-zero-framework-cognition.md'
- LABELS.md line 3: 'ZFC.md' — stays as 'ZFC.md' (both now at root, still sibling)
- prd/PRD-zero-framework-cognition.md: all occurrences of 'ortus/prompts/ZFC.md' and 'ortus/prompts/LABELS.md' → 'ZFC.md' and 'LABELS.md' (rg for exact occurrences; lines 188, 214, 231, 233, 260, 274 are known references).

**Parity:** scripts/check-ortus-parity.sh walks ortus/ and flags missing counterparts in template/ortus/. After the moves, neither file exists in ortus/prompts/ so parity won't flag them. The deleted template files become orphans the checker doesn't currently catch — that is fine since we are intentionally not mirroring these docs.
acceptance_criteria:
1. ZFC.md and LABELS.md exist at repo root; ortus/prompts/ZFC.md and ortus/prompts/LABELS.md no longer exist.
2. template/ortus/prompts/ZFC.md and template/ortus/prompts/LABELS.md no longer exist.
3. 'rg ortus/prompts/ZFC\.md' and 'rg ortus/prompts/LABELS\.md' return zero hits.
4. All relative links in the moved files resolve — verify by opening each Markdown link path and confirming the target exists.
5. 'make parity' exits 0.
6. 'copier copy --defaults --data project_name=testproj --data github_username=testuser template /tmp/test-ortus-move' still succeeds and generated project has no LABELS.md or ZFC.md in its prompts folder.
close_reason: Moved ZFC.md/LABELS.md to repo root (contributor docs, not runtime prompts); dropped template mirrors; updated internal links + PRD references; parity OK; copier smoke passes.

title: Replace 'bd activity' orient command with 'bd list --sort updated' in Ralph prompt
description:
Step 1 of the Ralph loop (Orient) currently runs:

    bd activity --limit 10 --json | jq -r '.[].issue_id' | sort -u | xargs -I{} sh -c 'echo "=== {} ===" && bd comments {} 2>/dev/null'

Replace with:

    bd list --sort updated --all --limit 10 --json | jq -r '.[].id' | xargs bd show --json

Locations to update:
- ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md:9
- template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja:9

Keep the two files byte-identical on that line (parity check will flag drift otherwise).
acceptance_criteria:
1. ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md:9 uses the new command verbatim.
2. template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja:9 uses the new command verbatim.
3. 'make parity' exits 0.
4. rg 'bd activity' returns no hits in ortus/prompts/ or template/ortus/prompts/.
close_reason: Replaced 'bd activity' orient command with 'bd list --sort updated | xargs bd show' in both canonical and template ralph prompt; parity clean.

title: Create ortus/prompts/LABELS.md documenting the label state machine
description: The interview workflow uses labels (interviewed, prd:interviewing, prd:ready, approved) for state tracking. Today this vocabulary is undocumented, so labels can drift into semantic routing over time. Create LABELS.md documenting each label's meaning and valid transitions — this makes the vocabulary an explicit schema (ZFC-compliant) rather than implicit semantic routing (forbidden). Parent epic: ortus-6ks. Source: PRD Epic 'Interview/PRD/AGENTS Cleanup' task bullet 2, FR-006.
design: Short doc — under 100 lines. Table format: Label | Meaning | Entry criteria | Exit criteria | Valid next labels. Lead with a one-paragraph statement of what the schema is for and what it's not for (the ZFC distinction). Cite the PRD audit row for interview.sh where this distinction originated.
acceptance_criteria: 1) File ortus/prompts/LABELS.md exists; 2) Documents each label (interviewed, prd:interviewing, prd:ready, approved) with its meaning; 3) Documents the valid state transitions (e.g. interviewed → prd:interviewing → prd:ready → approved); 4) Explicitly notes that these labels are a state schema, NOT a semantic-routing trigger — scripts may read them to track lifecycle but must NOT infer judgment from them; 5) States the rule: 'No new label values without updating this doc'; 6) Verification: a contributor reading LABELS.md alone can tell whether adding a new label like 'prd:draft' is needed and what transitions it implies
close_reason: LABELS.md written at ortus/prompts/LABELS.md (40 lines). Documents the interview/PRD label state machine (interviewed, prd:interviewing, prd:ready, approved) with meaning, entry/exit, valid transitions, and the schema-vs-semantic-routing rule. All 6 acceptance criteria verified. Template mirror tracked by ortus-2afi.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- Created ortus/prompts/LABELS.md (40 lines, <100 target) — label state machine schema for the interview/PRD workflow
- Lead paragraph establishes the ZFC distinction: labels are an explicit state schema (allowed), not a semantic-routing trigger (forbidden); cites PRD audit row for interview.sh (line 60)
- Table documents all 4 labels (interviewed, prd:interviewing, prd:ready, approved) with Meaning | Entry criteria | Exit criteria | Valid next labels; includes (none) starting state
- State transitions diagram and Rules section (schema-not-trigger; no new labels without updating doc; state changes are model-driven; labels are cumulative)
- Worked example walks through whether 'prd:draft' would be a needed label

**Verification**: wc -l → 40 (<100 ✓); all 4 labels present (11 total mentions ✓); schema/semantic distinction stated in lead and Rule 1 ✓; 'No new label values without updating this doc' rule present (line 28 ✓); worked example lets a contributor classify 'prd:draft' ✓. Template mirror handled by separate issue ortus-2afi.

title: Update Context Management for 1M context window
description:
Update the Context Management section in both prompt.md and template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja to acknowledge the 1M context window beta.

**Current first bullet (both files):**
```
- Fresh ~200K token window per invocation — this is your budget, spend it wisely
```

**New first bullet:**
```
- Fresh ~200K token window per invocation (1M available in beta for tier 4+ orgs) — 200K is the recommended default for Ralph loops; larger windows cost more and rarely improve single-task execution
```

**Rationale:** The 1M context window is available for Opus 4.6 with the `context-1m-2025-08-07` beta header. Premium pricing applies above 200K (2x input, 1.5x output). For Ralph one-task-per-invocation loops, 200K is sufficient and cost-optimal. But users should know the option exists.

title: Add Upstream/Downstream Steering section to both prompt files
description:
Add a new "Steering" section to both template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja and prompt.md explaining how the agent is steered from two directions.

**Upstream (issue descriptions are your spec):**
- The issue description is authoritative — implement what it says, not what you think it should say
- Follow existing code patterns found in src/ — match style, naming, structure
- Use shared utilities and existing abstractions before creating new ones
- If the issue is ambiguous, add a comment asking for clarification and output BLOCKED

**Downstream (tests/lints/builds are your guardrails):**
- Tests, lints, and builds reject invalid work — they are the final arbiter
- Iterate until passing — do not close an issue with failing checks
- Backpressure is a feature, not an obstacle — it tells you something is wrong
- If downstream checks reveal the issue spec is wrong, comment and BLOCKED

**Placement:** After the Subagent Strategy section, before Important Rules.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] ralph-prompt.md.jinja has Steering section with upstream and downstream subsections
- [ ] prompt.md has matching Steering section
- [ ] Section is placed logically (after Subagent Strategy, before Important Rules)
- [ ] Existing content is preserved unchanged

title: Patch prompt.md with ralph-beads commit 4aea9ca changes
description:
Apply changes from https://github.com/who/ralph-beads/commit/4aea9cad5cb01a90f7f8210a4908b4c9b832a032 to prompt.md.

This commit rewrites the Ralph loop prompt to prevent multi-task execution. Changes to apply:

## Step-by-step changes to prompt.md

### 1. Add preamble after line 3 (after 'Read @AGENTS.md...')
Add: 'You are invoked in a bash loop. Each invocation = one task. The loop restarts you with fresh context after you exit. Do ONE thing, then stop.'

### 2. Rename and restructure task steps
- Step 1: 'Check Recent Activity' → 'Orient' (same command)
- Step 2: 'Get Ready Work' → 'Select' (same EMPTY behavior, already present)
- Step 3: 'Claim Immediately' → 'Claim'
- NEW Step 4: 'Investigate' - 'Search the codebase first — don'\''t assume not implemented. Use subagents for broad searches.'
- Step 5: 'Implement' (unchanged)
- Step 6: 'Verify' - update wording: 'Run tests, linting, and builds (see Verification below). If they fail, fix and re-verify — this is backpressure, not a reason to stop.'
- Step 7: 'Log Completion' → 'Log' - 'Add structured completion comment (see format below)'
- Step 8: 'Complete' → 'Close' - same bd close command
- Step 9: Merge Commit+Push into one step 'Commit & Push' - 'Stage, commit with issue ID in message, then git pull --rebase && bd sync && git push'
- NEW Step 10: 'Exit' - 'Output the appropriate signal (see Completion Signals) and stop. You are done. The loop will restart you for the next task.'

### 3. Add BLOCKED escape hatch paragraph after step 10
'If you cannot complete the claimed issue (dependency, technical blocker, persistent test failure you cannot resolve), add a comment explaining the blocker via bd comments add <id> "...", then output <promise>BLOCKED</promise> and stop.'

### 4. Add backpressure paragraph to Verification section
After the existing verification text, add: 'If verification fails, fix the issue and re-verify. This is backpressure — keep iterating until it passes or you determine the issue is a blocker outside your task'\''s scope.'

### 5. Update Important Rules
- 'One issue per iteration' → 'One task per invocation - You will be restarted with fresh context for the next task. Do not run bd ready a second time. Do not claim a second issue.'
- 'No partial work' → 'No partial work - Either complete the issue fully or declare it BLOCKED'
- Remove 'Log completion' rule (redundant with step 7)
- Remove 'Run quality checks' rule (redundant with step 6)

### 6. Reorder Completion Signals to: EMPTY, COMPLETE, BLOCKED
Add closing line: 'After outputting any signal, stop immediately. Do not continue working.'

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Preamble added enforcing one-task-per-invocation model
- [ ] All 10 steps present with correct names and descriptions
- [ ] BLOCKED escape hatch paragraph present after step 10
- [ ] Backpressure guidance added to Verification section
- [ ] Important Rules updated (stronger one-task rule, BLOCKED in partial work, removed redundant rules)
- [ ] Completion Signals reordered with stop-immediately closing line
- [ ] No other content changed (Subagent Strategy, Ultrathink, Issue Type Rules, etc. remain as-is)
close_reason: Applied all 6 changes from ralph-beads commit 4aea9ca to prompt.md - all acceptance criteria verified

- prompt.md - Added BLOCKED escape hatch paragraph after step 10

title: Add Roblox game project type to Copier template
description:
Add support for creating Roblox game projects using the Ortus Copier template.

## Overview

Extend the template to support Roblox/Luau game development with:
- Luau as a language option
- Roblox-specific package managers (Wally, Rotriever)
- Roblox frameworks (Knit, Roact)
- Roblox-specific linting (selene, luau-lsp)
- Rojo-based external editor workflow

## Components to Modify

1. **copier.yaml** - Add Roblox project type, Luau language, package managers, frameworks, linters
2. **PRD template** - Create PRD-TEMPLATE-ROBLOX.md for game-specific concerns
3. **Interview prompt** - Add Roblox interview topics (game mechanics, multiplayer, monetization)
4. **Ralph prompt** - Add Luau verification commands
5. **CI workflow** - Add Roblox build/lint pipeline
6. **gitignore** - Add Roblox file patterns
7. **README template** - Add Roblox quick-start guide
8. **CLAUDE.md template** - Add Roblox development guidelines
9. **Project structure** - Add conditional Rojo project files and src layout

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Can select "roblox" as project_type in Copier
- [ ] Can select "Luau" as language

2. Ultrathink directive for complex problems
3. No placeholders rule - implement completely, no stubs
4. Found bugs rule: Always `bd create --type=bug` (never fix inline)

**Keep as-is:**

- bd ready → claim → implement → verify → comment → close → commit → push flow
- Issue type rules
- Completion signals (COMPLETE, BLOCKED, EMPTY)
- Neighboring task context remains implicit

**Do NOT add:**
- Git tagging (dropped)
- Update AGENTS.md rule

title: Improve PRD decomposition UX in idea.sh
description:
## Problem
The PRD intake flow in `idea.sh` has poor UX:

1. **Permission prompts**: Claude keeps asking for permissions to run `bd` commands despite `--allowedTools "Bash(bd *)"` being set
2. **No exit instruction**: User doesn't know to `/exit` Claude when decomposition is done
3. **Unclear next steps**: After exiting, the user should immediately know to:
   - `cd <projectdir>`
   - `./ortus/ralph.sh`

## Current Code (line 47)
```bash
echo "Read $prd_path. Use bd to create an epic and decompose into tasks with dependencies. Each task must have acceptance criteria." | claude --allowedTools "Read($prd_path),Bash(bd *)"
```

## Solution
1. **Fix permissions**: Investigate why `--allowedTools` isn't preventing prompts. May need different syntax or additional flags
2. **Improve prompt**: Add explicit instructions telling Claude to:
   - Work autonomously without asking for permissions
   - Tell the user to type `/exit` when done
   - (Optional) Provide a summary of what was created
3. **Simplify post-exit output**: Replace lines 53-56 with just two clear directions:
   ```
   ## Next Steps
   1. cd 
   2. ./ortus/ralph.sh
   ```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No permission prompts during PRD decomposition
- [ ] Claude tells user to `/exit` when done
- [ ] Post-exit output shows only the two essential next steps with a brief header
close_reason: Fixed --allowedTools syntax and simplified post-exit output

title: Refactor idea.sh to accept PRD path parameter
description:
Refactor idea.sh to accept an optional PRD path parameter instead of always asking for it interactively.

**Current behavior:**
idea.sh always prompts user to provide/paste a PRD interactively.

**New behavior:**
- idea.sh accepts an optional path argument: `./ortus/idea.sh [prd_path]`
- If path is provided, skip the PRD prompt and use that file directly
- If no path provided, fall back to interactive prompt (current behavior)
- When PRD is provided via copier (ortus-gfw), the _tasks will call idea.sh with the path to ./prd/intake.md

**Integration with copier:**
Update _tasks in copier.yaml:
```yaml
- "./ortus/idea.sh{{ ' prd/intake.md' if has_prd else '' }}"
```

This way:
- If user provided PRD during copier → idea.sh immediately starts splitting into tasks
- If no PRD provided → idea.sh prompts interactively as before
close_reason: Added --prd flag to idea.sh and updated copier.yaml to pass PRD path when available

title: Filter copier questions by selected language
description:
Make copier questions context-aware based on the selected language.

**Problem:**
Currently all package managers, frameworks, and linters are shown regardless of the selected language. If you pick Python, you still see Node.js options like npm, Next.js, ESLint.

**Solution:**
Use conditional `choices` or multiple questions with `when` clauses to show only relevant options:

**Python:**
- Package managers: uv, pip
- Frameworks: FastAPI, Flask, Django
- Linters: ruff

**TypeScript:**
- Package managers: bun, npm, pnpm, yarn
- Frameworks: Next.js, Express
- Linters: ESLint

**Go:**
- Package managers: go mod
- Frameworks: Gin
- Linters: golangci-lint

**Rust:**
- Package managers: cargo
- Frameworks: Actix, Axum
- Linters: clippy

**Implementation options:**
1. Use separate questions with `when` clauses per language
2. Use Jinja2 templating in `choices` if Copier supports dynamic choices
3. Use `validator` to reject invalid combinations
close_reason: Implemented language-aware conditional choices for package_manager, framework, and linter questions using Jinja2 templating

title: Add conditional PRD intake question to copier.yaml
description:

1. Ask 'Do you have a PRD?' (boolean)
2. If yes, ask for the path to the PRD file
3. Copy the PRD into the generated project's ./prd/ folder

Implementation:
```yaml
has_prd:
  type: bool
  help: Do you have a PRD (Product Requirements Document)?
  default: false

prd_path:
  type: str
  help: Path to your PRD file (will be copied into project)
  when: '{{ has_prd }}'
```

Add to _tasks to copy PRD into project:
```yaml
- "{{ 'cp \"' + prd_path + '\" prd/intake.md' if has_prd else 'true' }}"
```

The copied PRD will be available at ./prd/intake.md in the generated project.

title: Improve post-copier UX: user left in wrong directory
description:
## Problem

After running `copier copy gh:who/ortus ./foo --trust`, the user completes the wizard (including PRD intake via idea.sh), but is then left in their original directory (e.g., `~/code`) instead of the new project directory (`./foo`).

The final message says:
```
Next steps:
  bd ready           # See what's ready to work on
  ./ortus/ralph.sh   # Start implementing tasks
```

But running `./ortus/ralph.sh` from `~/code` fails because they're not in the project.

## Root Cause

Copier (and all subprocesses) cannot change the parent shell's working directory. This is a fundamental shell limitation.

## Proposed Fix

Update the messaging to include the project path explicitly:

```
Next steps:
  cd ./foo                  # Enter your new project
  bd ready                  # See what's ready to work on
  ./ortus/ralph.sh          # Start implementing tasks
```

Files to update:
- copier.yaml (`_message_after_copy`)
- template/ortus/idea.sh (final output message)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Post-copier message includes `cd <project-name>` as first step
- [ ] idea.sh output includes reminder of project directory if run from copier context
close_reason: Post-copier messages now include project directory path for cd

**Verification**: tests/test-copier.sh passes, post-copy message now shows cd path

title: Create ortus/README.md for generated projects
description:
## Task
Create a README.md in the ortus/ folder explaining what it is.

## Content
```markdown
# Ortus

This project was bootstrapped with [Ortus](https://github.com/who/ortus).

## What is Ortus?

Ortus is a project template that sets up:
- **Beads** (`bd`) - Local-first issue tracking
- **Ralph** - Autonomous task execution loop
- **Interview** - PRD generation through conversation

## Scripts

| Script | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `./ortus/ralph.sh` | Run the task implementation loop |
| `./ortus/idea.sh` | Quick-start a new feature |
| `./ortus/interview.sh` | Generate PRD through interview |
| `./ortus/tail.sh` | Watch ralph logs |

## Prompts

The `prompts/` folder contains instructions for Claude:
- `ralph-prompt.md` - Task execution instructions
- `interview-prompt.md` - PRD interview flow
- `prd-prompt.md` - PRD generation template
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] template/ortus/README.md.jinja created
- [ ] Links to ortus repo
- [ ] Explains purpose of each script
- [ ] Lists prompts and their purpose

title: Clean up interview completion: prompt user to exit and run ralph.sh
description:
## Task
Update the interview completion messaging to clearly prompt the user to exit Claude and run ./ralph.sh.

```
"The interview and PRD process is complete! I've created [N] implementation tasks for ralph. You can:
- Run \`./ralph.sh\` to start implementing the tasks
- Run \`bd ready\` to see available tasks
- Run \`bd show <task-id>\` to view task details

Please type \`/exit\` or press Ctrl+C to exit this Claude session."
```

## Desired Outcome
Make the exit prompt more prominent and actionable:
- Lead with the action: exit Claude, then run ralph.sh
- Remove noise (bd ready/bd show are secondary)
- Keep it punchy

## Example New Messaging
```
"All set! I've created [N] tasks ready for implementation.

**Next step**: Exit this session and run:
\`\`\`
./ralph.sh
\`\`\`

(Type /exit or Ctrl+C to leave)"
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Completion message leads with exit instruction
- [ ] ./ralph.sh is the clear next action
- [ ] Secondary commands (bd ready, etc.) removed or de-emphasized
- [ ] Tone is friendly and concise

title: Make prompt.md.jinja verification steps conditional on copier answers
description:
## Task
Update template/prompt.md.jinja so the Verification section uses more granular Copier conditionals based on user answers during project generation.

## Current State
The verification section (lines 18-65) uses basic language conditionals but:
- Assumes ruff for Python linting regardless of `linter` choice
- Assumes eslint for TypeScript regardless of `linter` choice
- Hardcodes mypy type checking for Python
- Doesn't account for `framework` specific test commands

## Required Changes
1. **Linter commands** - Use `linter` variable:
   - Show `ruff check` only if `linter == 'ruff'`
   - Show `eslint` only if `linter == 'eslint'`
   - Show `golangci-lint` only if `linter == 'golangci'`
   - Show `clippy` only if `linter == 'clippy'`
   - Omit linter line if `linter == 'none'`

2. **Framework-specific tests** - Consider framework:
   - Next.js projects might use `next lint` instead of eslint directly
   - Django might use `python manage.py test`
   - FastAPI with pytest is fine

3. **Type checking** - Make optional:
   - TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` is fine
   - Python mypy could be conditional if we add a mypy question, or just note it's optional

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Linter commands match the `linter` copier variable
- [ ] No linter command shown if `linter == 'none'`
- [ ] Template still generates valid markdown
- [ ] Existing language fallbacks preserved
close_reason: Implemented in commit 835e16e - verification steps now conditional on linter and framework choices

title: Remove activity.md from ortus project root
description:
Remove the activity.md file from the ortus project root.

## Rationale
The ralph-beads pattern uses ticket comments as the activity log instead of a separate activity.md file. This provides:
- Co-located context (activity is on the ticket, not separate file)
- Queryable via `bd activity` and `bd comments`
- No file to manually maintain

## Changes
1. Delete activity.md from ortus project root
2. The historical entries can be preserved in git history

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] activity.md removed from ortus root
- [ ] PROMPT.md no longer references activity.md (done in separate task)

title: Update PROMPT.md to match ralph-beads prompt.md
description:
Update PROMPT.md to match the ralph-beads prompt.md structure.

## Key Changes

1. **Remove activity.md references**
   - Step 4: Remove "Read @activity.md" reference
   - Step 10: Remove "Log Activity: Update activity.md with dated entry"

2. **Add bd activity for cross-loop visibility**
   - Step 1: Check recent activity via bd activity command
   - Add command: `bd activity --limit 10 --json | jq -r '.[].issue_id' | sort -u | xargs -I{} sh -c 'echo "=== {} ===" && bd comments {} 2>/dev/null'`

3. **Simplify bd ready usage**
   - Remove --assignee ralph filter (not needed if not using assignee filtering)
   - Use plain `bd ready --json` 

4. **Add Issue Type Rules section**
   - task: Implement exactly what's specified, no scope expansion
   - bug: Reproduce, diagnose, fix - no unrelated changes
   - epic/feature: Milestone check - close when all children complete

5. **Simplify completion comment format**
   - Single structured comment with Changes and Verification
   - Remove multi-step logging (Start, Progress, Verification)

6. **Add epic/feature handling**
   - Run `bd show <id>` to check children
   - If all children closed, close with reason
   - If children open, output BLOCKED

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No references to activity.md
- [ ] Uses bd activity for cross-loop visibility
- [ ] Has Issue Type Rules section
- [ ] Simplified ticket update to single completion comment
- [ ] Handles epic/feature milestone checks
close_reason: Updated PROMPT.md.jinja to match ralph-beads pattern with bd activity command, simplified logging, and completion comment format

title: Simplify ralph.sh to match ralph-beads pattern
description:
Replace the complex ralph.sh with the simpler ralph-beads version.

## Current State
- Has --tasks N, --iterations N, --idle-sleep options
- run_single_task() with iteration loops
- Complex task counting and limit checking

## Target State (from ralph-beads)
- Simple while loop with --idle-sleep option only
- No iteration limits - each Claude invocation handles one task
- Looks for COMPLETE or BLOCKED signals
- Exits when no more ready work

## Changes
1. Replace ralph.sh with simplified version from ralph-beads
2. Keep logs/ directory and timestamped logging
3. Remove --tasks and --iterations options
4. Keep --idle-sleep for continuous mode

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ralph.sh matches ralph-beads pattern
- [ ] --idle-sleep option preserved
- [ ] Logs to logs/ralph-<timestamp>.log
- [ ] Exits cleanly when no ready work

title: Epic: Update ortus to ralph-beads pattern
description:
Migrate the ortus project (NOT ./template directory) to use the simplified ralph-beads pattern from https://github.com/who/ralph-beads

## Key Changes

The ralph-beads pattern simplifies the workflow:
- **Simpler ralph.sh**: Single loop, no iterations-per-task complexity
- **Ticket comments as activity log**: Replace activity.md with `bd comments` 
- **Issue type rules inline**: task/bug/epic behavior defined in prompt.md
- **Cross-loop visibility via bd activity**: Check recent work with `bd activity` command

## Current State (ortus root)
- `ralph.sh` - Complex with --tasks, --iterations options
- `PROMPT.md` - References activity.md, has elaborate ticket guidelines
- `AGENTS.md` - Similar to target
- `activity.md` - Large manual activity log (should be replaced by bd comments)

## Target State (ralph-beads pattern)
- `ralph.sh` - Simple loop, runs until queue empty
- `prompt.md` - Issue type rules, bd activity for cross-loop visibility
- `AGENTS.md` - Clean landing-the-plane protocol
- No activity.md - Use `bd comments` on tickets instead

## Reference
Source: https://github.com/who/ralph-beads

title: Update ralph.sh to remove task-only filter
description:

ready_count=$(bd ready --assignee ralph --json | jq -r '[.[] | select(.issue_type == "task")] | length')
```

```

The PROMPT.md dispatch logic now handles type-specific behavior.

description:

title: Create prompts/epic.md workflow prompt
description:
Create the epic-type workflow prompt for ceremony-only handling.

**Workflow** (ceremony only - epics are never 'implemented'):
1. Epic becomes ready when all children are complete
2. Quality gate audit: verify acceptance criteria have corresponding completed tasks
3. If gates missing: create missing test/doc tasks, keep epic open
4. If gates pass: write retrospective to activity.md (key decisions/learnings)
5. Close epic with meaningful summary

**Note**: This integrates with ortus-wpb epic closure ceremony design.

**Acceptance criteria**:
- prompts/epic.md contains ceremony-only workflow
- Quality gate audit documented
- Retrospective format documented
- Can be @included from PROMPT.md

title: Create prompts/feature.md workflow prompt
description:
Create the feature-type workflow prompt with size-based behavior.

**Workflow**:
1. Assess feature complexity (LLM judgment)
2. If small enough to implement directly:
   - Implement like a task
   - Close with summary
3. If too large:
   - Decompose into child tasks with dependencies
   - Continue working on first task in same session

**Acceptance criteria**:
- prompts/feature.md contains size-assessment logic
- Small feature path documented
- Decomposition path documented (create and continue)
- Can be @included from PROMPT.md

title: Create prompts/bug.md workflow prompt
description:
Create the bug-type workflow prompt for debug-first bug handling.

**Workflow**:
1. Reproduce the bug (find steps to trigger it)
2. If cannot reproduce: mark BLOCKED, add comment requesting reproduction steps
3. Diagnose root cause
4. Implement fix
5. Add regression test (REQUIRED - cannot close without)
6. Verify fix resolves the issue
7. Close with summary

**Acceptance criteria**:
- prompts/bug.md contains debug-first workflow
- Regression test is documented as required gate
- Cannot-reproduce path documented
- Can be @included from PROMPT.md

title: Add type-specific workflow system to Ralph
description:
Ralph should handle different issue types with type-specific workflows.

## Architecture
- PROMPT.md remains the base prompt
- Type-specific prompts in prompts/ are @included based on issue type
- Ralph determines issue type from bd ready output, includes relevant workflow

## Type Behaviors

| Type | Behavior |
|------|----------|
| task | Implement directly (current) |
| bug | Debug-first: reproduce → diagnose → fix → regression test (required) → close |
| feature | LLM judges size. Small: implement. Large: decompose, then continue |
| epic | Ceremony only (quality gate + retrospective, per ortus-wpb) |
| chore | Same as task, but only when no other work ready |
| unknown | Log warning, skip |

## Key Rules
- Priority ordering: number only (P0 bug = P0 task)
- Feature decomposition: create tasks and continue in same session
- Bug test: required gate, cannot close without regression test
- Bug repro failure: block and request more info

title: Add epic closure ceremony to PROMPT.md
description:
When Ralph closes the last task under an epic/feature, perform a closing ceremony:

1. **Detect epic completion**: After closing a task, check if its parent epic now has all children completed
2. **Quality gate audit**: Review the epic's acceptance criteria and verify each has a corresponding completed task
3. **Block if gates missing**: If criteria aren't covered, create missing tasks and keep epic open
4. **Retrospective**: Write key decisions/learnings to activity.md
5. **Close epic**: Mark done with meaningful summary

This mirrors real-world milestone moments when significant work completes.

title: Add README.md generation and maintenance to template
description:
## Problem
Generated projects don't have a README.md, making it harder for users to understand the project structure and workflows.

## Requirements

### 1. Initial README Generation
Create a README.md.jinja template that includes:
- Project name and description (from copier answers)
- Tech stack summary (language, framework, package manager)
- Quick start guide (setup, run, test commands based on language)
- Workflow overview (idea.sh → interview.sh → ralph.sh)
- Basic bd commands reference
- Project structure overview

### 2. README Maintenance Instructions
Update PROMPT.md.jinja to instruct Ralph to update README.md after implementing major features:
- Add new feature documentation
- Update usage examples
- Keep existing user-edited sections intact
- Only update when changes are user-facing

title: Add PRD quality gate validation
description:
Add a validation step before PRD approval that checks for completeness and quality.

## Validation Checks
1. **Section completeness**:
   - Problem Statement present and >50 words
   - Success Metrics defined (at least 2)
   - Functional Requirements listed (at least 3)
   - Out of Scope section populated

2. **Quality indicators**:
   - Success metrics are measurable (contain numbers or clear criteria)
   - Requirements have acceptance criteria
   - No TODO/TBD placeholders remain
   - Open Questions section addresses unknowns

3. **Warnings** (non-blocking):
   - Thin sections (<30 words) flagged for review
   - Missing NFRs for certain project types (e.g., API should have security NFRs)
   - Large scope without phasing

## Implementation
- Add validation function in INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md after PRD generation
- Display validation results to user before approval prompt
- Allow user to proceed with warnings or request improvements

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Validation runs automatically after PRD generation
- [ ] Blocking issues prevent approval without override
- [ ] Warnings displayed but don't block
- [ ] User can request PRD improvements based on validation

title: Enhance INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md with adaptive questioning
description:
Improve the interview prompt to produce higher quality requirements through smarter questioning.

## Enhancements
1. **Adaptive depth**: Detect vague/short answers and probe deeper
   - If answer is <20 words, ask clarifying follow-up
   - Track which areas need more detail

2. **Project-type-aware questions**: Tailor questions based on project_type
   - API: Ask about endpoints, authentication, rate limits, data models
   - CLI: Ask about commands, flags, input sources, output formats
   - Library: Ask about public API surface, versioning strategy, compatibility
   - Fullstack: Ask about frontend/backend split, state management, deployment

3. **Confidence tracking**: Before generating PRD, assess completeness
   - Flag areas that seem underspecified
   - Offer to dig deeper on weak areas

4. **Expert mode**: Detect comprehensive answers and condense remaining questions
   - If user gives detailed answers, skip redundant questions
   - Respect user's time

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Interview adapts based on project_type
- [ ] Vague answers trigger follow-up questions
- [ ] Interview summary shows confidence level per area
- [ ] Works with existing interview.sh flow
close_reason: Enhanced INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md with adaptive questioning: project-type-aware questions (API/CLI/Library/Fullstack/Other), adaptive depth probing for vague answers, confidence tracking per area, and expert mode detection.

title: Add project_type selection to copier.yaml
description:
Add a project_type question to copier.yaml with options for different project archetypes.

## Options to add
- **api** - API service (endpoints, schemas, auth)
- **cli** - CLI tool (commands, flags, I/O)
- **library** - Library/package (public API, versioning)
- **fullstack** - Full-stack app (frontend + backend)
- **other** - Generic project (current behavior)

## Implementation
1. Add project_type question after framework selection
2. Default to 'other' for backwards compatibility
3. This value will drive:
   - Which PRD template is used
   - Which interview questions are asked

title: Add cost monitoring and caps for RLM usage
description:
## Task

Implement cost monitoring and configurable caps for RLM usage to prevent runaway costs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Track token usage per RLM call
- [ ] Track recursive sub-call depth and count
- [ ] Implement configurable cost caps
- [ ] Add warnings when approaching limits
- [ ] Log cost metrics for analysis

## Configuration

```yaml
rlm:
  max_tokens_per_call: 100000
  max_recursive_depth: 2
  max_sub_calls: 10
  cost_cap_usd: 5.00
```

## Risk Note

Paper notes: "Comparable cost at median, high variance at tail"
Caps are essential for production use.

## References

- analysis/RLM-integration-analysis.md Section 5.1

# After each file edit
verify_result = rlm.sub_call(

```

title: Add task batching to ralph.sh
description:
## Task

Modify ralph.sh to batch related tasks for more efficient implementation.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Detect related tasks (same epic, similar files, shared context)
- [ ] Group tasks into batches for RLM processing
- [ ] Implement batch execution mode in ralph.sh
- [ ] Maintain individual task tracking (bd update/close)
- [ ] Add --batch flag to enable/disable

## Technical Approach

```bash
# ralph.sh with batching
if [ "$RLM_ENABLED" = "true" ] && [ "$BATCH_MODE" = "true" ]; then
    tasks=$(bd ready --assignee ralph --json | group_related_tasks)
    python3 rlm_batch_impl.py "$tasks"
else
    # Current single-task behavior
fi
```

## Expected Speedup

2-3x for related task batches (shared context understanding)

## References

- analysis/RLM-integration-analysis.md Section 3.2
close_reason: Implemented task batching with epic/domain grouping, --batch flag, and RLM batch implementation script

title: Integrate RLM (Recursive Language Models) for workflow acceleration
description:
## Overview

Integrate the Recursive Language Models (RLM) paradigm from arXiv:2512.24601 into ortus-generated projects to accelerate the beads/ralph workflow.

## Background

RLM treats prompts as external environment variables that the model can programmatically inspect, decompose, and recursively process. This maps well to ortus's workflow where the codebase is the primary context.

See: analysis/RLM-integration-analysis.md for full technical analysis.

## Key Benefits

- **3-5x speedup** for task decomposition in interview.sh
- **2-3x speedup** for batched task implementation in ralph.sh
- **Automatic dependency analysis** (removes human bottleneck)
- **Higher quality PRDs** with codebase awareness

## Implementation Strategy

Opt-in enhancement with feature flag, starting with interview.sh as proof-of-concept.

## Success Criteria

- Task completion time reduced from ~5 min to ~2 min per task
- PRD revision cycles reduced from 2-3 to 1 average
- Dependency accuracy improved from ~70% to ~90%

## References

- Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.24601
- Implementation: https://github.com/alexzhang13/rlm
close_reason: All 13 implementation tasks completed. RLM integration added to ralph.sh (batching, planning, context sharing, verification, cost monitoring, sandbox, benchmarking) and interview.sh (codebase-aware task decomposition, automatic dependency detection, parallel bd create).

title: Add 'skip interview' option for one-shot PRD generation
description:
## Task

Add an initial question in interview.sh that asks the user if they want to skip the full interview and attempt a 'one-shot' PRD generation directly from the idea.

## First Interview Question

```
Question: Do you want to skip the interview and attempt a 'one-shot' PRD generation?
Header: Mode
Options:
  - 'No, run full interview (Recommended)' - Asks clarifying questions to build detailed requirements
  - 'Yes, one-shot' - Skip interview, generate PRD directly from idea (not recommended)
```

## Behavior

### If 'No' (full interview):
- Proceed with normal interview flow
- Ask all clarifying questions
- Generate PRD from answers
- Create tasks

### If 'Yes' (one-shot):
- Skip all interview questions
- Immediately generate PRD from just the feature title + description
- Show PRD for approval
- Create tasks if approved
- Warn user that quality may be lower without interview

## Implementation

Update INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md:
```
## First Question (Required)

Before asking any interview questions, first ask:

Use AskUserQuestion:
  Question: 'Do you want to skip the interview and attempt a one-shot PRD generation?'
  Header: 'Mode'
  Options:
    - label: 'No, full interview (Recommended)'
      description: 'Ask clarifying questions to build detailed requirements'
    - label: 'Yes, one-shot'
      description: 'Skip interview, generate PRD directly from idea (not recommended)'

If user selects 'Yes, one-shot':
  1. Warn: 'One-shot mode may produce lower quality PRDs. Proceeding...'
  2. Skip to PRD generation using only the feature title and description
  3. Show PRD and ask for approval
  4. Create tasks if approved

If user selects 'No, full interview':
  1. Proceed with normal interview questions
```

## Files to Modify
- `template/prompts/INTERVIEW-PROMPT.md` - Add first question logic

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] First question asks about skipping interview
- [ ] 'Recommended' label on full interview option
- [ ] One-shot mode skips to PRD generation
- [ ] Warning displayed for one-shot mode
- [ ] Full interview proceeds normally if selected

title: Add test for interview.sh AskUserQuestion flow
description:
## Task

Create a test to verify that interview.sh properly triggers AskUserQuestion when starting an interview. This ensures ortus-4ms does not regress.

## Test Scenario

1. Create a fresh project from Ortus template
2. Create a feature assigned to lisa
3. Run interview.sh
4. Verify Claude immediately uses AskUserQuestion (not waiting at prompt)

## Implementation

Add to `tests/test-interview.sh` or extend `tests/test-lisa.sh`:

```bash
# Test: interview.sh triggers AskUserQuestion
echo '=== Test: interview.sh AskUserQuestion flow ==='

# Create test feature
bd create --title="Test feature" --type=feature --assignee=lisa

# Run interview.sh with timeout, capture output
# Look for AskUserQuestion being invoked (tool call in output)
# Or check that Claude asks a question within N seconds

# Success criteria:
# - Claude starts asking questions without user prompt
# - AskUserQuestion tool is called
```

## Challenges
- Testing interactive Claude sessions is tricky
- May need to use `--print` mode or timeout-based detection
- Consider mocking or using Claude's non-interactive mode for testing

## Related Issues
- ortus-4ms (the bug this test validates)

## Files to Create/Modify

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Auto-run idea.sh in prompt mode after copier completes
description:
## Task

After copier finishes creating a project, automatically cd into the directory and run idea.sh in interactive/prompt mode to capture the user's first idea. The idea should be up-sampled into a full beads feature with description using Claude.

## Current Flow
1. copier copy gh:who/ortus ./myproject
2. User sees 'Next steps: cd myproject, ./idea.sh ...'
3. User must manually cd and run idea.sh

## Desired Flow
1. copier copy gh:who/ortus ./myproject
2. Copier finishes, user is IN the project directory
3. Automatically prompts: 'What's your idea?'
4. User types brief idea (e.g., 'calculator cli')
5. Claude up-samples into full feature with description
6. Feature created via bd create with rich description

## Up-sampling Example

User input: 'calculator cli'

Up-sampled feature:
```
Title: Calculator CLI application
Description: 
A command-line calculator that supports basic arithmetic operations 
(add, subtract, multiply, divide). Should handle user input, display 
results, and provide helpful error messages for invalid input.
```

## Implementation

### idea.sh with Claude up-sampling
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
if [[ -z "$1" ]]; then
    read -p "What's your idea? " idea
else
    idea="$1"
fi

# Use Claude to up-sample the idea into a full description
description=$(claude --print "Up-sample this brief idea into a 2-3 sentence feature description. Be concise and specific. Idea: $idea")

bd create --title="$idea" --type=feature --assignee=lisa --description="$description"
```

### Copier auto-cd
Research if copier can change the user's working directory after completion. Options:
- Shell function wrapper
- Post-copy hook that sources into current shell
- Instruct user to use: `cd $(copier copy ... --quiet)`

## Constraints
- Copier tasks run in subshells, can't change user's working directory directly
- May need wrapper script or shell function

## Files to Modify
- `template/idea.sh` - Add prompt mode + Claude up-sampling
- `copier.yaml` - Add task to run idea.sh, research auto-cd options
- Possibly add install script that creates shell function

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] idea.sh prompts for input when called with no args
- [ ] Claude up-samples brief idea into full description
- [ ] Feature created with rich description
- [ ] copier runs idea.sh after project creation
- [ ] Investigate/implement auto-cd into project directory
close_reason: Added Claude up-sampling to idea.sh - brief ideas expanded to rich descriptions

title: PrerequisiteChecker extension not loading during template generation
description:
## Summary

The PrerequisiteChecker extension in extensions/prerequisites.py is not being loaded or executed during template generation. The module's print statements never appear in output.

## Expected Behavior

When running `copier copy template /tmp/test`, the extension should print "Checking prerequisites..." and show tool availability status.

## Actual Behavior

The extension output never appears. Debug print statements added to module load time don't appear, indicating the module isn't being loaded at all.

## Technical Details

- extensions/prerequisites.py exists and is correctly formatted
- copier.yaml includes: `- extensions/prerequisites.py:PrerequisiteChecker` in _jinja_extensions
- The GitConfigContext extension in extensions/context.py uses the same pattern but its functionality works (git defaults appear)

## Possible Causes

1. ContextHook may not be triggered in certain Copier execution modes
2. The extension path may need to be absolute or differently specified
3. Copier may require --trust flag for extensions to load

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] PrerequisiteChecker runs during template generation
- [ ] Output shows tool availability checks with ✓/✗ markers
close_reason: Fixed: moved checks to __init__ with flush=True, output now appears at start of template generation

comments.author: who

comments.text: Progress: Found that the extension IS loading - output appears at the end after 'Project created'. The issue is timing - ContextHook runs during Jinja template rendering which happens late. Need to run checks earlier.

title: Implement lisa.sh approval and ralph handoff
description:
## Summary

When human adds 'approved' label, generate implementation tasks and assign to ralph.

## Requirements

- Detect 'approved' label on ideas with 'prd:ready'
- Read the generated PRD document
- Use Claude to break down PRD into implementation tasks
- Create tasks with --assignee=ralph
- Set up proper dependencies between tasks
- Close the original idea with a summary

## Task Creation

```bash
# For each epic/task from PRD breakdown:
bd create --title="[Task title]" \
  --type=task \
  --priority=2 \
  --assignee=ralph \
  --description="[Description with acceptance criteria]

Reference: prd/PRD-<name>.md"

# Set up dependencies
bd dep add <child-task> <parent-epic>
```

## Completion

- Remove prd:ready and approved labels
- Close the idea: `bd close <idea-id> --reason="PRD complete. Created X tasks for ralph."`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Detects 'approved' label
- [ ] Generates implementation tasks from PRD
- [ ] All tasks assigned to ralph
- [ ] Dependencies set up correctly
- [ ] Original idea closed with summary
- [ ] Log shows task creation details

title: Implement lisa.sh interview completion detection
description:
## Summary

Implement detection of when all interview questions have been answered (closed) by the human.

## Requirements

- Check if idea has 'prd:interviewing' label
- Query for child question beads that are still open
- If all question beads are closed → interview is complete
- Collect answers from question bead comments
- Transition idea from 'prd:interviewing' to 'prd:ready'

## Logic

```bash
# Get blockers for this idea
blockers=$(bd show $idea_id --json | jq '.blocked_by')
open_blockers=$(filter for status=open)

if [ open_blockers_count -eq 0 ]; then
    # All questions answered\!
    collect_answers $idea_id
    bd update $idea_id --remove-label prd:interviewing
    bd update $idea_id --label prd:ready
fi
```

## Answer Collection

- For each closed question bead, extract the comment(s) as the answer
- Compile into a structured format for PRD generation
- Store in memory or temp file for next phase

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Detects when all question beads are closed
- [ ] Collects answers from question bead comments
- [ ] Transitions label from prd:interviewing to prd:ready
- [ ] Handles edge cases (no questions, already closed)
close_reason: Implemented handle_interviewing() and collect_answers() functions with dependency detection, answer collection, and label transitions

title: Create lisa.sh core loop structure
description:
## Summary

Create the main lisa.sh script with the continuous loop structure, similar to ralph.sh.

## Requirements

- Continuous polling loop with configurable interval
- Logging to logs/lisa-<timestamp>.log
- Process ideas assigned to 'lisa' via `bd ready --assignee lisa`
- State machine based on labels (prd:interviewing, prd:ready, prd:approved)
- Graceful handling of errors

## Structure

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# lisa.sh - PRD generation and idea-to-implementation pipeline

POLL_INTERVAL=${1:-30}

# Setup logging
mkdir -p logs
LOG_FILE="logs/lisa-$(date '+%Y%m%d-%H%M%S').log"

log() { ... }

process_idea() {
    local idea_id="$1"
    # Check labels, route to appropriate handler
    # - No prd: label → start interview
    # - prd:interviewing → check if interview complete
    # - prd:ready → waiting for human approval
    # - prd:approved → generate tasks for ralph
}

# Main loop
while true; do
    ideas=$(bd ready --assignee lisa --json)
    # Process each idea
    sleep $POLL_INTERVAL
done
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] lisa.sh created with loop structure
- [ ] Logging works correctly
- [ ] Polls for ideas assigned to lisa
- [ ] State routing based on labels
- [ ] Handles empty queue gracefully
close_reason: Created lisa.sh with core loop structure: configurable polling interval, timestamped logging, state routing by prd:* labels, graceful empty queue handling.

## Current Problems

- test-ralph.sh is in the root directory, should be in tests/
- Task definitions are inline in the test script
- Tasks need clearer acceptance criteria and descriptions

## Required Changes

### 1. Create tests/ folder structure
```
tests/
├── test-ralph.sh          # Main test runner
└── fixtures/
    └── calculator-tasks.md  # Task definitions file
```

### 2. Create task definition file (tests/fixtures/calculator-tasks.md)

Define 3 simple tasks with clear descriptions and acceptance criteria. Example:

```markdown
# Task 1: Create greeting module
Create a file src/greeting.py with a greet() function that returns 'Hello, World!'

## Acceptance Criteria
- File src/greeting.py exists
- Function greet() is defined
- greet() returns the string 'Hello, World!'

---

# Task 2: Add personalized greeting
Add a greet_person(name) function to src/greeting.py that returns 'Hello, {name}!'

## Acceptance Criteria
- Function greet_person(name) is defined in src/greeting.py
- greet_person('Alice') returns 'Hello, Alice!'

---

# Task 3: Add farewell function
Add a farewell(name) function to src/greeting.py that returns 'Goodbye, {name}!'

## Acceptance Criteria
- Function farewell(name) is defined in src/greeting.py
- farewell('Bob') returns 'Goodbye, Bob!'
```

### 3. Update test-ralph.sh

- Move to tests/test-ralph.sh
- Read task definitions from tests/fixtures/calculator-tasks.md
- Create beads tasks in the tmp test project's beads context
- Parse the markdown file to extract title, description, and acceptance criteria for each task

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] tests/ folder exists with test-ralph.sh
- [ ] tests/fixtures/calculator-tasks.md defines 3 simple tasks
- [ ] Tasks have clear descriptions and acceptance criteria
- [ ] test-ralph.sh reads from the fixture file
- [ ] Tasks are created in the tmp project's beads context (not ortus's)
- [ ] Old test-ralph.sh removed from root
close_reason: Refactored test-ralph.sh into tests/ folder with external fixture file for task definitions

title: Create test suite for ralph.sh with deterministic project
description:
## Summary

Create an automated test suite that verifies ralph.sh behavior with the new --tasks N option using a deterministic test project.

## Test Setup

1. Generate a test project using the template with Python defaults
2. Create 3 simple, deterministic beads tasks (e.g., create a file, add a function, add a test)
3. Tasks should be simple enough that Claude can complete them reliably

## Test Cases

### Test 1: ralph --tasks 1
- Run `./ralph.sh --tasks 1`
- Verify exactly 1 task was completed (closed)
- Verify 2 tasks remain open

### Test 2: ralph (unlimited)
- Run `./ralph.sh` (no task limit)
- Verify all remaining tasks are completed
- Verify 0 tasks remain open

## Implementation Notes

- Use a clean /tmp directory for each test run
- Tasks should be idempotent and verifiable (e.g., check file exists, function exists)
- Consider using `bd list --status=closed --json | jq length` to count completed tasks
- May need to mock or stub Claude calls for true determinism, or accept some flakiness

## Example Deterministic Tasks

```bash
bd create --title="Create src/calculator.py with add function" --type=task --assignee=ralph
bd create --title="Add subtract function to calculator.py" --type=task --assignee=ralph  
bd create --title="Add multiply function to calculator.py" --type=task --assignee=ralph
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Test script exists (e.g., test-ralph.sh or tests/test_ralph.py)
- [ ] Test generates fresh project from template
- [ ] Test creates 3 deterministic tasks
- [ ] Test verifies --tasks 1 completes exactly 1 task
- [ ] Test verifies unlimited ralph completes all remaining tasks
- [ ] Tests can be run via a single command
close_reason: Created test-ralph.sh with dry-run mode for setup verification; full test runs actual Claude API

title: Update ralph.sh log tailing instructions to mention tail.sh
description:
## Summary

Update template/ralph.sh to improve the log tailing instructions shown to users. Currently it just mentions tailing the log file, but should offer two options:

1. **Human-readable output**: Use tail.sh with no arguments - it automatically tails all new additions to files in the logs folder
2. **Raw output**: Use standard tail -f for a specific log file

## Changes Needed

In template/ralph.sh, where it mentions tailing logs, update to something like:

```
To follow along:
  Human-readable: ./tail.sh
  Raw output:     tail -f logs/<logfile>
```

## Note on tail.sh behavior

tail.sh should automatically watch the logs/ folder and tail all new content from any log files, requiring no arguments from the user.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ralph.sh shows both tail.sh (no args) and tail -f options when mentioning log following
- [ ] Instructions are clear about the difference (human-readable auto-follows all logs vs raw single file)
- [ ] tail.sh example shows no arguments needed
close_reason: Fixed ralph.sh to show tail.sh without arguments (auto-follows all logs)

title: Add tail.sh to template with terminal-agnostic colors
description:
## Summary

Move tail.sh into the template folder so it's included in generated projects, and make its color output work well across different terminal themes (light/dark).

## Tasks

1. **Move tail.sh to template/** - Copy tail.sh into template/ folder so it gets included in generated projects

2. **Add to chmod in _tasks** - Update copier.yaml _tasks to make tail.sh executable

3. **Fix terminal color compatibility** - The current color codes may not display well on all terminal themes (light vs dark backgrounds). Options:
   - Use semantic ANSI colors (e.g., default foreground/background) instead of specific RGB values
   - Detect terminal background and adapt colors
   - Use only bold/dim/underline styling without colors
   - Use tput for terminal-capability-aware color codes
   - Offer a --no-color flag

## Research Needed

- Review current color codes in tail.sh
- Determine which colors are problematic on light vs dark themes
- Choose best approach for cross-terminal compatibility

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] tail.sh exists in template/ and is copied to generated projects
- [ ] tail.sh is made executable via _tasks in copier.yaml
- [ ] Colors are readable on both light and dark terminal themes
- [ ] Fallback works gracefully if terminal doesn't support colors
close_reason: Moved tail.sh to template/, added tput-based terminal-aware colors with NO_COLOR support, added to chmod task in copier.yaml

title: Run prerequisite checks without adding shell script to generated project
description:
## Summary

Currently check-prerequisites.sh is copied into the generated project and run as a _tasks step. We want to run prerequisite checks during copier generation without leaving a shell script in the generated project.

## Current Behavior

- check-prerequisites.sh is copied to the generated project
- It's run as a _tasks step: `./check-prerequisites.sh`
- The script remains in the project after generation

## Desired Behavior

- Prerequisite checks run during copier generation
- No check-prerequisites.sh file left in the generated project

## Possible Approaches

1. **Copier extension (Python)** - Add a Jinja extension or context that runs checks and reports results
2. **_tasks with inline commands** - Run checks inline in _tasks without a script file
3. **Post-generation hook** - Use copier's task system to run checks from the template directory (not copied)
4. **Add to _exclude** - Keep script in template/ but exclude from copy, reference by full path in _tasks

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Prerequisite checks still run during project generation
- [ ] No check-prerequisites.sh in the generated project
- [ ] Clear error messages if prerequisites missing
- [ ] Works on Linux/macOS
close_reason: Implemented prerequisite checks as Copier extension instead of shell script

title: Use git config for default author_name and author_email in copier wizard
description:
## Summary

When running the copier wizard, the author_name and author_email prompts should default to the user's git config values if available.

## Current Behavior

- author_name defaults to "Developer"
- author_email defaults to "dev@example.com"

## Expected Behavior

- author_name should default to $(git config --global user.name) if available, else "Developer"
- author_email should default to $(git config --global user.email) if available, else "dev@example.com"

## Implementation

In copier.yaml, use Jinja2 expressions with shell commands to fetch git config values:

```yaml
author_name:
  type: str
  help: Your name
  default: "{{ 'git config --global user.name' | shell | trim or 'Developer' }}"

author_email:
  type: str
  help: Your email
  default: "{{ 'git config --global user.email' | shell | trim or 'dev@example.com' }}"
```

Note: Need to verify copier supports the `shell` filter. If not, may need an alternative approach.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Running copier shows git username as default for author_name (if git configured)
- [ ] Running copier shows git email as default for author_email (if git configured)
- [ ] Falls back to "Developer" / "dev@example.com" if git not configured
- [ ] Test with and without git config set
close_reason: Implemented git config defaults using copier-template-extensions ContextHook

title: PRD pipeline creates tasks without assigning to ralph
description:
When creating a new project via the PRD pipeline, the generated shell script creates beads tasks but does not assign them to ralph. This causes the ralph loop to have no tasks to work on, requiring manual intervention to assign all tasks to ralph.

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Use the PRD pipeline to create a new project
2. Complete the interview process
3. Observe the generated bd create commands in the shell script
4. Note that tasks are created without --assignee=ralph

**Expected behavior:**
Tasks created by the PRD pipeline should be automatically assigned to ralph so they appear in ralph's work queue.

**Actual behavior:**
Tasks are created unassigned, causing ralph loop to have no work.
close_reason: Fixed by adding explicit CRITICAL reminders in generate-prd-from-interview.sh and PRD-PROMPT.md that all bd create commands MUST include --assignee=ralph

title: Create prd-pipeline.sh orchestration script
description:
## Overview
Create the main orchestration script that ties together all PRD pipeline components into a continuous loop.

## Requirements

### Main Loop Structure
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# prd-pipeline.sh - Automated PRD generation pipeline

while true; do
    # 1. Check for ideas assigned to prd-pipeline
    # 2. Process each idea through the pipeline
    # 3. Sleep and repeat
done
```

### Idea Intake
- Run `bd ready --assignee prd-pipeline` to find ideas ready for processing
- Filter to only process ideas (not tasks/epics)
- Track which ideas are already being processed (avoid duplicate processing)

### Pipeline States
Handle ideas in different stages:

1. **New idea (no Q&A subtasks)**: 
   - Generate Q&A subtasks (use task copier-project-archetype-65m logic)
   - Mark as "in Q&A phase"

2. **In Q&A phase (has open subtasks)**:
   - Check if all Q&A subtasks are closed
   - If not, skip (waiting for human)
   - Could log status for visibility

3. **Q&A complete (all subtasks closed)**:
   - Collect answers (use task copier-project-archetype-5ic logic)
   - Generate PRD and explode tasks (use task copier-project-archetype-8rx logic)
   - Close idea

### State Tracking
- Use bead labels or a local state file to track pipeline phase
- Possible labels: `prd:qa-pending`, `prd:qa-complete`, `prd:generating`
- Or store state in `.prd-pipeline-state.json`

### Error Handling
- Catch and log errors for each idea
- Don't let one failed idea block others
- Consider retry logic with backoff

### Logging
- Log pipeline activity for debugging
- Show which ideas are being processed
- Report on Q&A waiting status

## Configuration
- POLL_INTERVAL: How often to check for new ideas (default: 60s)
- DRY_RUN: Option to simulate without making changes

## File Location
- Create at: `scripts/prd-pipeline.sh`
- Make executable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Continuously polls for prd-pipeline assigned ideas
- [ ] Correctly identifies idea pipeline stage
- [ ] Orchestrates Q&A generation for new ideas
- [ ] Detects Q&A completion and triggers PRD generation
- [ ] Handles errors gracefully
- [ ] Includes logging for observability
- [ ] Can be run as background process
close_reason: Done: Created prd-pipeline.sh orchestration script with full async workflow

title: Implement PRD generation and task explosion
description:
## Overview
Generate the PRD document from collected Q&A answers and explode it into implementation tasks assigned to ralph.

## Requirements

### PRD Generation
- Input: Original idea + collected Q&A pairs
- Use prd/PRD-PROMPT.md as the generation template
- Generate comprehensive PRD document including:
  - Problem statement
  - Goals and success metrics
  - User stories
  - Technical requirements
  - Implementation phases
  - Open questions/risks

### PRD Output
- Save to: `prd/PRD-[name].md`
  - [name] derived from idea title (slugified)
- Include metadata header with:
  - Source idea ID
  - Generation date
  - Q&A summary

### Task Explosion
After PRD generation:
- Parse the PRD for implementation phases/tasks
- Create beads for each major work item:
  - Epics for large phases
  - Tasks for specific implementations
- All created beads should be:
  - Assigned to ralph (--assignee ralph)
  - Linked as dependencies or children appropriately
- Reference the PRD document in task descriptions

### Idea Closure
- After successful PRD generation and task creation
- Close the original idea: `bd close <idea-id>`
- Add closing comment summarizing what was created

## Implementation Notes
- Use Claude to generate PRD content with prd/PRD-PROMPT.md
- Consider using existing generate-prd.sh as reference
- Task explosion should be intelligent about granularity

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Generates well-structured PRD document
- [ ] Saves PRD to correct location with proper naming
- [ ] Creates implementation tasks from PRD
- [ ] Tasks are properly assigned to ralph
- [ ] Original idea is closed with summary
close_reason: Done: Created generate-prd-from-qa.sh for PRD generation and task explosion

title: Implement Q&A completion detection and answer collection
description:
## Overview
Implement logic to detect when all Q&A subtasks are completed and collect the answers for PRD generation.

## Requirements

### Completion Detection
- Query subtasks of an idea: `bd list --parent <idea-id>`
- Check status of each Q&A subtask
- Determine if ALL subtasks are closed/completed
- This can be used in a polling loop or triggered by dependency resolution

### Answer Collection
When all Q&A subtasks are closed:
- Iterate through each subtask
- Extract answers from:
  - Subtask description (if human edited it)
  - Subtask comments (use `bd comments <id>`)
- Compile answers into structured format:
  ```
  Q1: [original question]
  A1: [collected answer]
  
  Q2: [original question]
  A2: [collected answer]
  ...
  ```

### Output Format
- Return structured data suitable for PRD generation:
  - Original idea description
  - List of Q&A pairs
  - Any additional context/metadata

## Implementation Notes
- Create as reusable function for prd-pipeline.sh
- Handle case where subtask has no answer (warn but continue)
- Consider timeout/staleness detection (optional: warn if Q&A open too long)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Correctly detects when all Q&A subtasks are closed
- [ ] Extracts answers from subtask descriptions
- [ ] Extracts answers from subtask comments
- [ ] Compiles structured Q&A output
- [ ] Handles missing/empty answers gracefully
close_reason: Done: Created collect-qa.sh for Q&A completion detection and answer collection

title: Create Q&A subtask generation from idea descriptions
description:
## Overview
Implement the logic to read an idea's description and generate discovery question subtasks using prd/PRD-PROMPT.md.

## Requirements

### Input Processing
- Read the idea bead's description field
- Parse and understand the idea context

### Question Generation
- Use prd/PRD-PROMPT.md as the prompt template for generating discovery questions
- Generate 3-7 focused questions that help clarify:
  - User needs and pain points
  - Scope and constraints
  - Success criteria
  - Technical considerations
  - Edge cases

### Subtask Creation
For each generated question:
- Create a subtask using `bd create -t task --parent <idea-id>`
- Set assignee to human (or configurable)
- Title should be the question itself (truncated if needed)
- Description should include:
  - Full question text
  - Context from the original idea
  - Instructions: "Answer by editing this description or adding a comment, then close when done"

### Dependency Setup
- Each Q&A subtask should block the parent idea: `bd dep add <idea-id> <subtask-id>`
- This ensures the idea cannot be closed until all Q&A is complete

## Implementation Notes
- Create as a function/module that can be called from prd-pipeline.sh
- Use `bd show <id> --json` to read bead details
- Return list of created subtask IDs for tracking

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Reads idea description successfully
- [ ] Generates relevant discovery questions using PRD-PROMPT.md
- [ ] Creates subtasks with proper structure
- [ ] Sets up blocking dependencies correctly
- [ ] Handles edge cases (empty description, etc.)
close_reason: Done: Created generate-qa.sh and PRD-QA-PROMPT.md for Q&A subtask generation

title: Upstream: bd init should chmod existing .beads/ to 0700 on first use
description:
Follow-up to ortus-mlgu. The Ortus template now chmods .beads/ to 0700 in copier _tasks, but this only fixes Ortus-generated projects. Other entry points (git clone, manual mkdir, copier without our task) still produce 0755 dirs that bd warns about on every call.

Better long-term fix: 'bd init' should chmod an existing .beads/ to 0700 on first use, not only when it creates the directory itself. This benefits all bd users, not just Ortus.

Action: file an issue on the bd (beads) upstream repo describing the desired behavior. Include a reproduction (mkdir .beads with default umask, then bd init, then bd ready — observe warning) and the proposed fix (chmod on existing dir during init or doctor).
close_reason: Filed upstream bd issue gastownhall/beads#3391 with confirmed reproduction and proposed fix

title: Add ralph.sh --fast flag for Claude 4.6 fast mode
description:
Add an optional --fast flag to template/ortus/ralph.sh and ralph.sh that enables fast mode when invoking Claude.

**Claude 4.6 fast mode:** 2.5x faster output tokens/second for Opus, same model and intelligence. Premium pricing ($30/$150 per MTok = 6x standard). Requires beta header `fast-mode-2026-02-01`.

**Current invocation (template/ortus/ralph.sh line 38):**
```bash
result=$(claude -p "$(cat "$(dirname "$0")/prompts/ralph-prompt.md")" --output-format stream-json --verbose --dangerously-skip-permissions 2>&1 | tee -a "$LOG_FILE") || true
```

**Changes needed:**
1. Add SPEED="" variable at top
2. Add --fast flag to argument parsing: `--fast) SPEED="--fast"; shift ;;`
3. Log the speed setting if fast mode enabled
4. Pass $SPEED to the claude invocation (if Claude Code CLI supports a --fast flag)

**Important:** Check whether Claude Code CLI currently supports a --fast or --speed flag. If it does not yet, implement the flag parsing anyway but document that it is a no-op until CLI support lands. Add a comment in the script.

**Apply same changes to both:**
- template/ortus/ralph.sh (template)
- ralph.sh (ortus project itself)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] template/ortus/ralph.sh accepts --fast flag
- [ ] ralph.sh accepts --fast flag  
- [ ] Flag is documented in script header usage line
- [ ] If CLI does not support fast mode yet, flag is parsed but a warning is logged
- [ ] Default behavior (no --fast) is unchanged
close_reason: Implemented --fast flag in both ralph.sh and template/ortus/ralph.sh. Flag is parsed, logged, and passed to claude CLI invocation.

comments.author: who
comments.text:

- ralph.sh - Added --fast flag parsing, FAST_MODE variable, fast mode log message, and pass-through to claude CLI invocation
- template/ortus/ralph.sh - Same changes applied to template version
- Both usage lines updated to document --fast flag

**Verification**: bash -n syntax check passes on both files. Template generation (copier copy) succeeds and generated ralph.sh contains --fast flag. Claude CLI accepts --fast without error (tested with --fast --help). Default behavior (no --fast) unchanged — FAST_MODE expands to empty string.

title: Add LLM-as-Judge note in Verification section of ralph-prompt.md.jinja

**Content to add (after the existing verification commands, before Issue Type Rules):**

For subjective quality criteria (UX polish, documentation clarity, creative output) where automated tests cannot provide a pass/fail signal, use LLM-as-Judge: spawn a subagent to evaluate the output against the acceptance criteria with a binary pass/fail verdict. Treat a "fail" verdict the same as a failing test — iterate until it passes.

title: Add compaction safety net guidance to Context Management
description:
Add a brief note about the Compaction API to the Context Management section in both prompt.md and template/ortus/prompts/ralph-prompt.md.jinja.

**Context:** The Compaction API (beta, Opus 4.6) provides server-side context summarization when tokens exceed a threshold. For Ralph one-task-per-invocation loops this is rarely needed, but for tasks with massive tool output (e.g., large test suites, extensive codebase exploration) it can prevent context exhaustion within a single invocation.

**Add after the existing Context Management bullets:**
```
- If a single task generates massive tool output approaching the context limit, the Compaction API can summarize earlier turns automatically — but this is rare with well-scoped tasks
```

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] prompt.md Context Management section has compaction note as the last bullet
- [ ] ralph-prompt.md.jinja Context Management section has matching compaction note
- [ ] Existing bullets unchanged
- [ ] No other content modified
